findings.title: Unauthenticated proxy to Qiwa government API (/api/qiwa)
findings.description: The REST route apps/web/app/api/qiwa/route.ts exposes GET, POST and PUT handlers that acquire a Qiwa OAuth token and proxy the request straight to the Qiwa government API (api.qiwa.sa / sandbox). No auth() session check, no role check, no tenant scoping and no Zod validation are performed. The path is also NOT included in middleware.ts config.matcher, so the global rate-limit and same-origin CSRF checks never run for it. The request body is forwarded verbatim to the Qiwa endpoint (JSON.stringify(body)) and the employee id comes from an unvalidated ?id= query param. A properly gated tRPC equivalent (qiwaRouter, requireRole(...QIWA_MANAGE_ROLES)) already exists, so this is an unprotected parallel door to the same integration.
findings.expectedBehavior: Every call to a government-integration endpoint must require an authenticated session, enforce the qiwa manage roles, be tenant-scoped, validate the payload with the existing qiwaEmployeeSchema, and be covered by rate limiting.
findings.actualBehavior: Any unauthenticated caller can POST/PUT arbitrary employee data to Qiwa or GET employee records, using the server's own Qiwa credentials, with no session, role, tenant, validation or rate limit.

evidence.snippet: export const config = { matcher: [ "/api/auth/callback/credentials", ... "/api/trpc/:path*", ... ] };  // /api/qiwa is absent -> no middleware, no rate-limit, no CSRF

findings.reproduction: curl -X POST https://<host>/api/qiwa -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"fullName":"x"}'  (no cookie/session) -> request is proxied to Qiwa and the raw Qiwa response is returned.
findings.technicalRisk: Server-side abuse of stored Qiwa credentials; unvalidated payload passed to an external government API; SSRF-adjacent proxy behaviour; internal error text leaked to the caller.
findings.businessRisk: Fraudulent creation/modification of employee/contract records in the Saudi government Qiwa system under the tenant's identity; regulatory and legal exposure; credential abuse.
findings.recommendation: Add auth() + role check (QIWA_MANAGE_ROLES) at the top of every handler, resolve tenant from the session, validate the body with qiwaEmployeeSchema/qiwaContractSchema, add /api/qiwa to middleware.matcher for rate-limit + CSRF, and return generic error messages. Preferably retire this REST route in favour of the already-gated tRPC qiwaRouter.
findings.workaround: Block /api/qiwa at the edge/WAF or delete the route until authentication is added.

findings.title: Unauthenticated cross-tenant data exposure (/api/auth/check-tenants)
findings.category: Broken Access Control / Excessive Data Exposure
findings.description: apps/web/app/api/auth/check-tenants/route.ts GET returns the 10 most recently created tenants (id, companyName, crNumber, nitaqatActivity, planTier, regulatoryContext, schemaName) and the 10 most recent users (id, email, name, role, tenantId) with NO authentication. This is the same registry data that the tRPC procedure auth.tenantsList deliberately gates behind a PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist (SEC-002) and which even omits schemaName. The route is not in middleware.matcher, so it is not even rate limited.
findings.expectedBehavior: Cross-tenant registry data (customer company names, Saudi CR numbers, internal Postgres schema names, and user emails/roles) must only be readable by an authenticated platform operator, matching the auth.tenantsList gate.
findings.actualBehavior: Anyone on the internet can enumerate customer companies, CR numbers, internal schema names, and user emails/roles across ALL tenants by requesting one URL.

evidence.snippet: tenantsList: protectedProcedure.query(async ({ ctx }) => { const allowedOperators = (process.env.PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS ?? "").split(",")...; if (!email || !allowedOperators.includes(email)) throw new TRPCError({ code: "FORBIDDEN" }); // gated equivalent

findings.reproduction: curl https://<host>/api/auth/check-tenants  -> JSON dump of recent tenants (incl crNumber + schemaName) and users (incl email + role).
findings.technicalRisk: Discloses internal Postgres schema-per-tenant names (aids further attacks), user emails and roles, and tenant identifiers with zero authentication.
findings.businessRisk: PII leak (emails), disclosure of customer identities and Saudi commercial-registration (CR) numbers across the whole platform; multi-tenant confidentiality breach; PDPL exposure.
findings.recommendation: Delete this diagnostic route, or gate it behind the same PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist used by auth.tenantsList and add it to middleware.matcher. Never return schemaName to any client.
findings.workaround: Remove the route file immediately; it is redundant with auth.tenantsList.

findings.title: Tenant provisioning DDL creates only 2 of ~24 module tables — new tenants are structurally broken
findings.exploitability: Not an attack — triggered by normal signup. Every new tenant hits it.
findings.description: generateTenantDDL() emits CREATE TABLE for only `departments` and `employees`. The tenant schema module defines ~24 tables (payroll_runs, payslips, wage_files, expenses, leave_types/requests/balances, attendance shifts/records/exceptions, documents, compliance_checks, final_settlements, notifications, audit_logs, recruitment, retention, ai, qiwa_contracts, policy_documents, guide_maps, employee_invitations, employment_history). None of the other tables are created when a tenant is provisioned at runtime.
findings.expectedBehavior: createTenantSchema() should materialize the complete, current tenant table set (every table exported from schema/tenant/index.ts) with all constraints, indexes and triggers, so all modules work for a freshly signed-up tenant.
findings.actualBehavior: Only departments + employees are created. Payroll, leave, attendance, expenses, recruitment, audit, compliance etc. tables do not exist for a new tenant, so those modules fail with 'relation does not exist' until someone hand-runs a seed/DDL script.

evidence.snippet: export { departments } ...; export { employees } ...; export { payrollRuns } from './payroll_runs'; export { payslips } ...; export { auditLogs } ...; /* ~24 tables total */
*   PHASE 1 (DDL)  — creates any MISSING tenant tables (CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ...)

findings.reproduction: Call createTenantRegistry(company, cr, activity) (the production signup path) then connect to the new tenant_<hex> schema and \dt. Only departments and employees exist. Any payroll/leave/attendance query throws 42P01 relation does not exist.
findings.technicalRisk: Every module beyond org-chart is non-functional on new tenants; runtime errors, failed writes, no audit capture.
findings.businessRisk: A prospect who self-signs-up gets a broken product. Data captured before tables exist is lost. Undermines the multi-tenant onboarding story entirely.
findings.recommendation: Generate tenant DDL from the Drizzle schema (single source of truth) rather than a hand-maintained string. Options: (a) drive drizzle-kit against each new tenant schema via SET search_path; (b) build a per-tenant migration runner that replays an authoritative ordered DDL bundle covering all tables + constraints + indexes + triggers; (c) at minimum, extend generateTenantDDL to the full table set and add a test asserting parity with schema/tenant/index.ts exports.
findings.workaround: Run scripts/seed-rukn-modules.ts (PHASE 1 DDL) against each new tenant manually.
findings.estimatedEffort: L (2-4 days: author authoritative per-tenant DDL/migration mechanism + parity test)

findings.title: Migrations 0001 & 0002 hardcode a single dev tenant schema (tenant_1ed8b6bd3743) — changes never reach other or new tenants
findings.exploitability: Not attacker-driven; deterministic drift affecting all tenants except one hardcoded schema.
findings.description: Migration 0001 (immigration + gcc columns) and 0002 (policy_documents table + indexes) are written against a literal schema name `tenant_1ed8b6bd3743`. They ALTER/CREATE only inside that one developer tenant. Any other existing tenant and every future tenant never receives these columns/tables. Contrast with 0006/0007/0009 which correctly loop over information_schema.schemata LIKE 'tenant\_%'.
findings.expectedBehavior: Schema migrations must apply to every tenant schema (loop over all tenant_<hex> schemas) and be baked into new-tenant provisioning.
findings.actualBehavior: 0001/0002 mutate exactly one schema. Employees.immigration_status / visa_type / iqama_expiry / passport_expiry / occupation_code / skill_level / gcc_status and the policy_documents table exist only in tenant_1ed8b6bd3743.

evidence.snippet: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenant_1ed8b6bd3743.policy_documents ( ... );

findings.reproduction: grep -roE 'tenant_[a-f0-9]{12}' packages/db/drizzle -> all hits are tenant_1ed8b6bd3743 in 0001 and 0002. Provision a second tenant, query employees.immigration_status -> column does not exist.
findings.technicalRisk: App code that reads immigration_status/visa_type/policy_documents throws for all tenants but one. Silent divergence between canonical Drizzle schema and live tenant schemas.
findings.businessRisk: Right-to-work / iqama-expiry payroll blocking (a core Saudi compliance feature) is inoperative for real tenants; policy document module missing.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite 0001/0002 as DO-loops over all tenant_<hex> schemas (as 0006/0007/0009 do) AND fold the columns/tables into the authoritative provisioning DDL from DB-001. Add a CI check that fails on any literal tenant_<hex> reference in migrations. Also fix the date-vs-text mismatch (see DB-005).
findings.workaround: Manually replay the ALTERs against every existing tenant schema.

findings.title: Unauthenticated cross-tenant registry disclosure via GET /api/auth/check-tenants (re-opens SEC-002, leaks schemaName)
findings.description: The custom route GET /api/auth/check-tenants returns the ENTIRE tenant registry (companyName, crNumber, nitaqatActivity, planTier, regulatoryContext AND internal Postgres schemaName) plus the 10 most recent users across ALL tenants (email, name, role, tenantId) with NO authentication, no role check and no tenant scoping. This is the exact data SEC-002 was remediated for on the tRPC procedure auth.tenantsList (now gated to PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS with schemaName removed) — but this parallel REST door bypasses that fix entirely and additionally re-exposes schemaName, the internal isolation key SEC-002 explicitly removed. The path is not in the middleware matcher, so no rate-limit or edge guard applies either.
findings.expectedBehavior: Cross-tenant registry data must be restricted to a true platform operator (same PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS boundary used by auth.tenantsList) and must never expose schemaName. Ideally this diagnostic route should be deleted.
findings.actualBehavior: Any anonymous HTTP client receives every tenant's company name, CR number, plan tier and internal schemaName plus recent user emails/roles/tenantIds.

evidence.snippet:
regulatoryContext: t.regulatoryContext,
        schemaName: t.schemaName,
        createdAt: t.createdAt,

matcher: [
    "/api/auth/callback/credentials",
    "/api/auth/signup",
    "/api/auth/request-reset",
    "/api/auth/reset",

findings.reproduction: curl https://<host>/api/auth/check-tenants -> 200 JSON listing all tenants (incl. schemaName) and recent users across every tenant, no cookie/token supplied.
findings.technicalRisk: Full cross-tenant registry disclosure including the internal schema-per-tenant isolation key; enables targeting of other tenants' schemas and user enumeration.
findings.businessRisk: Saudi PDPL personal-data breach, competitor intelligence (every customer's CR number, plan tier), and exposure of the isolation primitive. Directly negates the SEC-002 remediation the 90/100 score relied on.
findings.recommendation: Delete apps/web/app/api/auth/check-tenants/route.ts. If a diagnostic is required, gate it on the same PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS operator boundary as auth.tenantsList and never return schemaName. Add the path to the middleware matcher regardless.
findings.workaround: Block /api/auth/check-tenants at the edge/CDN until removed.

findings.title: Unauthenticated cross-tenant disclosure of users and tenants via GET /api/auth/check-tenants
findings.category: Broken Access Control / Sensitive Data Exposure
findings.description: The check-tenants route handler performs no authentication or authorization and is not covered by the middleware matcher, so any anonymous caller can enumerate the 10 most recent tenants (company name, CR number, schema name, plan tier, regulatory context) and the 10 most recent users (email, name, role, tenantId) across the ENTIRE registry - a cross-tenant PII and infrastructure-metadata leak.
findings.expectedBehavior: A registry-wide listing of tenants and users must require an authenticated platform-operator (the same PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist gate used by authRouter.tenantsList), and must never be reachable anonymously.
findings.actualBehavior: GET /api/auth/check-tenants returns tenant + user records with no session check. The route is absent from middleware.ts `config.matcher`, so even the middleware never runs for it.

evidence.snippet:
users: recentUsers.map((u: any) => ({
        id: u.id,
        email: u.email,
        name: u.name,
        role: u.role,
        tenantId: u.tenantId,

matcher: [
    "/api/auth/callback/credentials",
    "/api/auth/signup",
    "/api/auth/request-reset",
    "/api/auth/reset",

findings.reproduction: curl https://<host>/api/auth/check-tenants -> returns JSON with cross-tenant tenants[] (companyName, crNumber, schemaName) and users[] (email, name, role, tenantId), no auth required.
findings.technicalRisk: Leaks internal Postgres schema names (aids schema-per-tenant targeting) and the full user email/role map, enabling targeted phishing, credential-stuffing and tenant reconnaissance.
findings.businessRisk: PDPL/GDPR-class personal-data breach: customer employee emails and names exposed publicly. Reputational and regulatory exposure for a Saudi HR SaaS.
findings.recommendation: Delete the route or gate it behind the platform-operator allowlist (reuse the PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS check from trpc/routers/auth.ts tenantsList) and add it to the middleware matcher. Return 404 to anonymous callers.
findings.workaround: Block /api/auth/check-tenants at the edge/CDN until fixed.

findings.title: Expense self-approval: submitter can set themselves as the approver and approve their own claim
findings.role: department_manager, hr_specialist, payroll_admin, recruiter (any non-employee staff role)
findings.description: createExpenseSchema accepts a client-supplied approverEmployeeId with no check that it differs from the submitter. The approve mutation authorises the row's approverEmployeeId (or any HR role). A submitter who sets approverEmployeeId to their own employee id therefore becomes the approver of their own expense and can approve it. Plain 'employee' role is blocked from expense.approve by canAccessProcedure, but every staff role above employee (department_manager, hr_specialist, payroll_admin, recruiter) reaches approve via protectedProcedure.
findings.expectedBehavior: An expense approver must be a different person from the submitter (actor != target). A self-referential approver should be rejected at submission, and approve must refuse when approverEmployeeId === the row's employeeId.
findings.actualBehavior: No actor != target guard exists. approverEmployeeId is taken verbatim from input; approve only checks that the caller is the assigned approver or HR, so a self-assigned approver passes.

evidence.snippet: approverEmployeeId: uuidSchema.optional().describe("Line manager who should approve; defaults to the employee's manager at submission time"),

findings.reproduction: As a department_manager, call expense.create with approverEmployeeId = own employeeId and amount = 5000. Then call expense.approve { id, action: 'approve' }. The row moves to 'approved' with no second person involved.
findings.technicalRisk: State transitions to 'approved' with no independent actor; no compensating audit record.
findings.businessRisk: Fraudulent reimbursement: an employee with a staff role can approve and (see BIZ-002) pay their own expenses up to the 1,000,000 SAR schema cap without any second signer.
findings.recommendation: Reject approverEmployeeId === submitter at create; in approve, additionally block when existing.employeeId === existing.approverEmployeeId or when the acting HR user is the submitter. Enforce a two-person rule for any monetary approval.
findings.workaround: Restrict approve/markPaid to a dedicated finance role and monitor the audit log for same-actor submit+approve pairs (not currently logged — see BIZ-003).

findings.title: audit_logs table and its append-only trigger are not part of tenant provisioning — new tenants have no audit trail and no tamper protection
findings.exploitability: An app-role compromise on a new tenant could write/alter sensitive data with zero audit record.
findings.description: generateTenantDDL does not create audit_logs, so new tenants have no audit table at all. Migration 0007 installs the append-only BEFORE UPDATE/DELETE trigger only for schemas that ALREADY contain audit_logs, and runs once at migration time. A tenant created after 0007 was applied therefore gets neither the table nor the trigger. The trigger design itself is correct and idempotent, but it is not wired into provisioning.
findings.expectedBehavior: Every tenant should be created with audit_logs plus the prevent_audit_mutation() trigger so audit entries are immutable from day one.
findings.actualBehavior: New tenants: no audit_logs table (audit writes fail or are skipped). Even if the table were added later by a script, the append-only trigger is not re-installed for tenants created after 0007.

evidence.snippet: generateTenantDDL() returns DDL for departments + employees only; no audit_logs.

-- Only act on schemas that actually have the audit_logs table.
IF EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = s AND table_name = 'audit_logs' ) THEN
export const auditLogs = pgTable('audit_logs', { id, userId, action, entityType, entityId, oldValue, newValue, ipAddress, createdAt });

findings.reproduction: Provision a new tenant; \dt shows no audit_logs. Add the table by hand and note no audit_logs_no_update/no_delete trigger exists -> UPDATE/DELETE on audit rows succeeds.
findings.technicalRisk: No immutable audit trail for sensitive operations on new tenants; tamper protection absent.
findings.businessRisk: Fails PDPL / SEC audit-log requirements; sensitive-operation logging promised in CLAUDE.md security rules is not enforced for real tenants.
findings.recommendation: Add audit_logs (with actor/action/target/before/after/ip/timestamp) and the prevent_audit_mutation() trigger to the authoritative provisioning DDL (DB-001). Keep 0007 as the backfill for legacy schemas. Add an integration test asserting UPDATE/DELETE on audit_logs raises in a freshly provisioned tenant.
findings.workaround: Run the 0007 DO-block after every tenant creation.

findings.title: Provisioned employees table is missing immigration/gcc columns the app requires, and migration types (text) diverge from schema (date)
findings.exploitability: Not attacker-driven; runtime failures on immigration/right-to-work features for new tenants.
findings.description: The Drizzle employees schema defines immigration columns (passportExpiry, iqamaExpiry, exitReentryExpiry as `date`; visaType, occupationCode, skillLevel, immigrationStatus, gccStatus). generateTenantDDL omits all of these. Migration 0001 adds them ONLY to tenant_1ed8b6bd3743 (see DB-002) AND declares passport_expiry/iqama_expiry/exit_reentry_expiry as `text`, conflicting with the `date` type in the canonical schema. So new tenants lack the columns; the one hardcoded tenant has them with the wrong type.
findings.expectedBehavior: Provisioned employees table matches employees.ts exactly, including immigration columns with correct `date` types.
findings.actualBehavior: New tenants: columns absent. Hardcoded tenant: columns present but typed text where Drizzle expects date -> type coercion / comparison bugs for expiry logic.

evidence.snippet: passportExpiry: date('passport_expiry'), iqamaExpiry: date('iqama_expiry'), exitReentryExpiry: date('exit_reentry_expiry'), visaType: visaTypeEnum(...), immigrationStatus: immigrationStatusEnum(...).default('valid')
ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS passport_expiry text; ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS iqama_expiry text; ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS exit_reentry_expiry text;

findings.reproduction: Provision a new tenant, query employees.iqama_expiry -> column missing. On tenant_1ed8b6bd3743, inspect column type -> text, not date.
findings.technicalRisk: Expiry-window comparisons on text dates are string comparisons; payroll-blocking on expired iqama/passport is unreliable or errors.
findings.businessRisk: Core Saudi right-to-work compliance (payroll block on expired iqama, 90/60/30-day alerts) broken or absent.
findings.recommendation: Include immigration/gcc columns with correct date/enum types in the authoritative provisioning DDL; align 0001 to date types; backfill/convert text->date on the affected schema.
findings.workaround: Manually add columns (as date) to affected tenant schemas.

findings.title: Dashboard shell uses hardcoded physical left/padding-left, so layout does not mirror in RTL (Arabic)
findings.wcagCriterion: 1.3.2 Meaningful Sequence (related); no direct SC for layout mirroring, but breaks the app's own documented RTL contract
findings.description: globals.css states the intent explicitly: "RTL: every component is RTL-first; dir=\"rtl\" on <html> flips physical properties ... so layouts mirror automatically." DashboardShell does correctly toggle document.documentElement.dir/lang at runtime (dashboard-shell.tsx:35-36), but the shell's own layout uses hardcoded physical Tailwind classes instead of logical ones: the sidebar wrapper is pinned with `left-0` and the content column uses `md:pl-[278px]`. Neither responds to `dir="rtl"`.
findings.expectedBehavior: When preferredLanguage is switched to Arabic (dir=rtl), the sidebar should move to the visual right edge and the main content column should reserve space on the right (e.g. via `inset-inline-start-0` / `ps-[278px]` logical utilities, or `[dir=rtl]:` overrides), matching the RTL-first design intent documented in globals.css.
findings.actualBehavior: The sidebar stays pinned to the physical left of the viewport and main content keeps its left padding even after switching to Arabic, producing a left-docked nav in an otherwise right-to-left reading page.

evidence.snippet: <div className="fixed inset-y-0 left-0 z-40 hidden md:block">
<div className="min-h-screen md:pl-[278px]">
RTL: every component is RTL-first; dir="rtl" on <html> flips physical properties (margin-inline-start, padding-inline-end) so layouts mirror automatically.

findings.reproduction: 1. Sign in to the dashboard. 2. Click the language toggle (Globe icon) in the header to switch to العربية. 3. Observe the sidebar remains on the left and main content padding is unchanged, rather than mirroring to the right.
findings.technicalRisk: Layout primitives don't use Tailwind logical properties (ps-/pe-/start-/end-), so the RTL contract only holds for components authored after this pattern was adopted, not the shell itself.
findings.businessRisk: Arabic-speaking users (the primary market — Saudi Arabia) get a visually inconsistent, unpolished experience on every single screen, undermining the product's "Saudi-luxury" positioning and potentially eroding trust in a govt/enterprise HR context.
findings.recommendation: Replace `left-0`/`pl-[278px]` (and similar physical positioning in header.tsx, employees list search icon, etc.) with Tailwind logical-property utilities (`start-0`, `ps-[278px]`) or add `[dir=rtl]:right-0 [dir=rtl]:pr-[278px]` overrides, then visually QA every dashboard page in Arabic.
findings.workaround: None for end users; Arabic users can still complete tasks, just with a mirrored-incorrectly nav rail.

findings.title: Language toggle on the login page is non-functional (dead button)
findings.wcagCriterion: N/A (UX/Nielsen: user control and freedom; consistency and standards)
findings.description: The login page renders what looks like a working EN/Arabic toggle in the top bar, but the "العربية" element is a `<button>` with no `onClick` handler at all, and "EN" is a plain `<span>`, not an interactive control. Every other authenticated page in the app has a working language toggle (header.tsx uses `setPreferredLanguage`), so this looks like an oversight specific to the pre-login screen.
findings.expectedBehavior: Clicking "العربية" switches the login page (and ideally the whole pre-auth flow) to Arabic/RTL, consistent with the working toggle used everywhere post-login.
findings.actualBehavior: Clicking the "العربية" button does nothing; there is no state, no dir change, and no visual feedback of any kind.

evidence.snippet:
<button type="button" className="rounded-full px-3 py-1 transition hover:bg-white/10 hover:text-white">
  العربية
</button>
EN

findings.reproduction: 1. Visit /login while signed out. 2. Click "العربية" in the top-right corner. 3. Nothing happens — page stays in English/LTR.
findings.technicalRisk: None beyond dead code; low complexity fix (wire to the same Lang state pattern used in regulatory-context.tsx).
findings.businessRisk: First impression for Arabic-speaking prospects/employees is a broken control before they even reach the product — particularly damaging for a Saudi-market SaaS whose demo/sales narrative leans on bilingual polish.
findings.recommendation: Wire the login-page toggle to the same `Lang`/dir-switching mechanism used in DashboardShell, or at minimum route users to a `?lang=ar` variant of the login form with translated copy.
findings.workaround: Users can complete login in English and switch language after signing in.

findings.title: Custom /api/auth/login is a brute-force oracle (no rate limit, no Zod)
findings.description: apps/web/app/api/auth/login/route.ts performs a raw bcrypt password comparison and returns the user's id/email/role/tenantId. It only checks that email and password are present (no Zod, no email-format or length bounds). The middleware rate limiter and per-account lockout are keyed on /api/auth/callback/credentials (the NextAuth path); this custom /api/auth/login route is NOT in middleware.matcher, so it has no rate limiting and no account lockout - a standalone unthrottled password-checking oracle.
findings.expectedBehavior: All credential-verification endpoints should be rate limited per IP/account, enforce account lockout, and validate input. Ideally there should be a single authentication path (NextAuth).
findings.actualBehavior: Attackers can brute-force passwords against /api/auth/login at full speed with no throttling or lockout; generic error text is good but the endpoint still confirms valid credentials and returns role/tenantId.

evidence.snippet: const isLoginAttempt = pathname.startsWith("/api/auth/callback/credentials");  // does NOT cover /api/auth/login

findings.reproduction: Loop POST /api/auth/login with a fixed email and a password list; observe no 429 throttling and no lockout after many attempts.
findings.technicalRisk: Unlimited offline-speed password guessing against live accounts; no lockout integration.
findings.businessRisk: Account takeover of HR/super_admin accounts leading to full tenant payroll/PII compromise.
findings.recommendation: Either remove this route in favour of NextAuth, or add it to middleware.matcher with the login rate-limit bucket, wire it to the same per-account lockout used in authorize(), and validate the body with a Zod schema (emailSchema + bounded password).
findings.workaround: Add /api/auth/login to middleware.matcher and reuse the login:<ip> rate-limit key.

findings.title: employee.getById / employee.list over-select salary + PII and lack object-level scoping
findings.description: employee.getById and employee.list run on companyProcedure, which only excludes the employee and candidate roles - recruiter and department_manager are admitted. Both return the whole employees row (no column projection), including plaintext salaryBasic/salaryHousing/salaryTransport and the *_enc PII columns (iqamaNumberEnc, passportNumberEnc, bankIbanEnc). Unlike employee.list (which department-scopes a department_manager), employee.getById performs NO department scoping, so a department_manager or recruiter can fetch ANY employee's full record - including salary - by iterating UUIDs. This overlaps RBAC-004 (recruiter/department_manager must not see salary) but is reported here as a data-layer over-selection + missing object-level authorization issue.
findings.expectedBehavior: Reads should project only the columns the caller is entitled to (salary and *_enc PII stripped for recruiter/department_manager) and getById should enforce the same department scoping as list.
findings.actualBehavior: getById returns the entire employee row (salary + encrypted PII + payslips relation) to any company role with no department scoping; a recruiter sees salary they are explicitly not permitted to see.

evidence.snippet:
getById: companyProcedure.input(z.string().uuid()).query(async ({ ctx, input }) => {
    return await ctx.db.query.employees.findFirst({ where: eq(schema.tenant.employees.id, input), with: { department: true, manager: true, documents: true, leaveRequests: true, payslips: true } }); });  // no dept scope, whole row

export const companyProcedure = protectedProcedure.use(({ ctx, next }) => { const role = ctx.user.role; if (role === "employee" || role === "candidate") throw new TRPCError({ code: "FORBIDDEN" }); return next({ ctx }); });  // recruiter + department_manager admitted

findings.reproduction: As a recruiter or department_manager, call trpc employee.getById with any employee UUID -> response includes salaryBasic/salaryHousing/salaryTransport and *_enc columns; the target need not be in the manager's department.
findings.technicalRisk: Data-layer over-selection of sensitive columns; object-level authorization missing on getById (IDOR across departments).
findings.businessRisk: Salary confidentiality breach and PII exposure to roles that must not see them; PDPL/compliance impact.
findings.recommendation: Add a column allowlist (Drizzle columns projection) that strips salary and *_enc fields for non-payroll roles, and apply getManagedDepartmentIds scoping to employee.getById as is already done in employee.list. Coordinate with the RBAC owner (RBAC-004).
findings.workaround: Restrict getById/list to the salary-authorized roles until per-column projection is added.

findings.title: Orphan POST /api/auth/login is an unthrottled password-verification oracle (no rate limit, no lockout, no MFA)
findings.description: A second, custom credentials endpoint exists alongside the NextAuth flow. It validates the password with bcrypt but is NOT in the middleware matcher (so the 5/min login rate limit never applies), does NOT consult or increment the durable account-lockout state, and does NOT enforce MFA. It therefore permits unlimited online password guessing against real accounts and, on success, discloses the account role and tenantId. The frontend uses signIn()/callback/credentials instead, so this route is unreferenced dead code that still serves live traffic.
findings.expectedBehavior: All credential-checking endpoints must share the same brute-force controls (IP rate limit + durable per-account lockout + MFA gate) as the NextAuth authorize() path, or not exist.
findings.actualBehavior: The route calls bcrypt compare and returns 200/401 with no throttling, no lockout accounting, and no MFA. It is missing from middleware.ts matcher entirely.

evidence.snippet:
const isLoginAttempt = pathname.startsWith("/api/auth/callback/credentials");
  if (isLoginAttempt && !checkRateLimit(`login:${ip}`, 5, 60_000)) {

findings.reproduction: Loop POST /api/auth/login with {email, password} candidates - no 429 and no account lockout is ever triggered; a 200 confirms a valid credential pair and reveals role/tenantId.
findings.technicalRisk: Bypasses every brute-force mitigation the team built for the NextAuth path; enables password spraying and credential stuffing at full speed.
findings.businessRisk: Account takeover of HR/payroll admins, leading to PII and payroll fraud.
findings.recommendation: Delete this route (the app authenticates via NextAuth). If a JSON login API is genuinely needed, route it through the same lockout + rate-limit + MFA logic and add it to the middleware matcher.
findings.workaround: Add /api/auth/login to the middleware matcher with the login rate limit, or block it at the edge.

findings.title: Monthly leave accrual compounds: year-to-date days are added to the existing balance on every run
findings.description: calculateAccrual computes daysAccrued = (daysAllowed/12) * monthsElapsed, where monthsElapsed = min(12, currentMonth+1) is the YEAR-TO-DATE month count, then sets newBalance = previousBalance + daysAccrued. Because the YTD figure is ADDED to the already-accrued balance every time the job runs, running the monthly cron each month compounds the balance to daysAllowed * (1+2+...+12)/12 = daysAllowed * 6.5 by December (e.g. 21 days -> ~136 days). Correct only if run exactly once per year.
findings.expectedBehavior: A monthly accrual run should add one month's entitlement (daysAllowed/12) to the balance, and be idempotent per month (no double-credit if re-run).
findings.actualBehavior: It adds the YTD accrual to the prior balance, so periodic runs over-accrue geometrically.

evidence.snippet: let newBalance = previousBalance + daysAccrued;  // adds YTD accrual on top of prior balance
const results = runMonthlyAccrual(leaveTypeContexts, employeeContexts, balanceContexts, { runForYear: targetYear });  // cron re-runs monthly

findings.reproduction: Seed balance 0. Run runMonthlyAccrual in Feb (monthsElapsed=2) -> balance = daysAllowed*2/12. Run again in Mar (monthsElapsed=3) -> balance = prev + daysAllowed*3/12. Balance grows by the YTD amount each month.
findings.technicalRisk: Non-idempotent accrual; balance diverges from entitlement.
findings.businessRisk: Every employee's leave balance is inflated across all tenants, inflating paid-leave usage and accrued-leave-payout liability at settlement.
findings.recommendation: Make the monthly path grant exactly one month (daysAccrued = monthlyAccrual) and add idempotency keyed to (employee, leaveType, year, month) so a re-run in the same month is a no-op; add unit tests asserting balance == entitlement after 12 monthly runs.
findings.workaround: Run the accrual job at most once per year until fixed; verify balances manually.

findings.title: iqama unique index and performance indexes are one-time backfills, not part of provisioning — new tenants can store duplicate national IDs and run unindexed
findings.exploitability: Duplicate iqama/national IDs can be inserted on new tenants if the app-layer check is bypassed or races.
findings.description: The partial unique index employees_iqama_unique (0009) and all hot-path indexes (0006) are applied by one-time DO-loops over existing schemas. generateTenantDDL creates employees.iqama_number_enc as plain TEXT with no unique index and none of the perf indexes. New tenants therefore lack DB-level duplicate-iqama protection and lack indexes on notifications, employees, leave_*, documents, payroll, payslips, expenses, final_settlements, compliance_checks, departments, attendance.
findings.expectedBehavior: Provisioning should create the iqama partial unique index and the standard performance indexes for every tenant.
findings.actualBehavior: Only tenants existing when 0006/0009 ran have them; new tenants have neither.

evidence.snippet: EXECUTE format('CREATE UNIQUE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS employees_iqama_unique ON %I.employees (iqama_number_enc) WHERE iqama_number_enc IS NOT NULL;', s);
iqama_number_enc TEXT,   -- created without a unique index
FOR schema_rec IN SELECT schema_name FROM information_schema.schemata WHERE schema_name ~ '^tenant_[a-f0-9]{12}$' LOOP

findings.reproduction: Provision a new tenant; \di shows no employees_iqama_unique and no perf indexes. Insert two employees with the same iqama via raw SQL -> both succeed.
findings.technicalRisk: Duplicate national IDs (payroll/GOSI keyed on iqama); full-table scans degrade as tenant grows.
findings.businessRisk: Duplicate-employee / duplicate-payroll risk; performance regressions for growing tenants.
findings.recommendation: Fold all indexes (incl. the iqama partial unique index) into the authoritative provisioning DDL (DB-001). Keep 0006/0009 as legacy backfills.
findings.workaround: Replay 0006 and 0009 DO-blocks after each tenant creation.

findings.title: employees table has zero explicit indexes despite being filtered by departmentId/employmentStatus/managerEmployeeId on nearly every query
findings.page: Employees list, Departments, Attendance, Leave, Payroll (any screen scoped by employee/department)
findings.description: packages/db/src/schema/tenant/employees.ts defines the employees table with no index()/uniqueIndex() calls at all. This table is the most heavily filtered/joined table in the app: employee.ts filters on departmentId and employmentStatus and sorts by fullName/hireDate/createdAt (apps/web/trpc/routers/employee.ts:18-37); attendance.ts filters employees by departmentId (line 459) and joins via managerEmployeeId for org-tree walks; leave.ts filters active employees by employmentStatus (line 337); departments.ts joins on headEmployeeId. Postgres does not auto-index foreign-key columns (unlike the primary key), so every one of these WHERE/JOIN clauses does a sequential scan once tenant employee counts grow past a few hundred rows. Only 7 of 33 schema files (packages/db/src/schema/**) define any explicit index at all; employees.ts and leave_requests.ts, expenses.ts, documents.ts, payroll_runs.ts, payslips.ts, notifications.ts are all in the no-index list.
findings.expectedBehavior: Foreign-key / frequently-filtered columns (departmentId, employmentStatus, managerEmployeeId) should have explicit btree indexes so list/report queries scale with tenant size.
findings.actualBehavior: No index definitions exist on the employees table or on most other tenant tables that are filtered by employeeId/departmentId in routers.

evidence.snippet: export const employees = pgTable("employees", { id: uuid(...).primaryKey(), departmentId: uuid(...).references(() => departments.id, ...), managerEmployeeId: uuid(...), ... }); // no index()/uniqueIndex() anywhere in file
if (input?.status) conditions.push(eq(schema.tenant.employees.employmentStatus, input.status));
if (input?.departmentId) conditions.push(eq(schema.tenant.employees.departmentId, input.departmentId));

findings.reproduction: Static evidence only — grep packages/db/src/schema for `index(`: only attendance.ts, shift-related and 6 other files define indexes; employees.ts, leave_requests.ts, expenses.ts, documents.ts, payroll_runs.ts, payslips.ts, notifications.ts have none.
findings.technicalRisk: Sequential scans on core tenant tables as employee/leave/expense/document/payslip counts grow; query latency degrades non-linearly with tenant size.
findings.businessRisk: Slower dashboards and reports for larger customer tenants; risk grows directly with the company's success (more employees = slower app), which is a bad first impression during demos/scale-up.
findings.recommendation: Add btree indexes on employees.departmentId, employees.employmentStatus, employees.managerEmployeeId, and the employeeId foreign key on leave_requests/expenses/documents/payslips/notifications; regenerate a Drizzle migration (pnpm db:generate) rather than hand-editing SQL.
findings.workaround: None at scale currently deployed (demo tenant is small so this is not yet visible in production).

findings.title: Tenant-isolation regression tests are mostly skipped, not executing
findings.description: The vitest run shows packages/db/src/__tests__/tenant-isolation.test.ts as "4 tests | 3 skipped" and packages/db/src/__tests__/tenant-manager.test.ts as "5 tests | 4 skipped" — i.e. the majority of the tests specifically named for verifying cross-tenant data isolation are not actually executing in the current run (likely gated behind a live-DB precondition, consistent with the known-broken local DATABASE_URL noted in project memory).
findings.expectedBehavior: Tenant-isolation tests are the most important regression guard in a multi-tenant SaaS (a prior RBAC audit already scored this area 90/100 after remediation) and should run on every CI build, not be silently skipped.
findings.actualBehavior: 7 of 9 combined tests across the two tenant-isolation-related suites are skipped in this run.

evidence.snippet: ✓ packages/db/src/__tests__/tenant-manager.test.ts (5 tests | 4 skipped) 15ms
✓ packages/db/src/__tests__/tenant-isolation.test.ts (4 tests | 3 skipped) 28ms

findings.reproduction: cd hrms-app && pnpm test — observe the skipped counts in the summary line for these two files.
findings.technicalRisk: If these skips are due to a missing live-DB connection (consistent with the local DATABASE_URL being broken per project memory), the same skip condition may also apply in CI unless CI provisions a real Postgres instance — meaning the most safety-critical tests in the repo may not be gating merges.
findings.businessRisk: A regression that leaks data across tenants (the single worst failure mode for this SaaS) could ship without the dedicated tests for it ever running.
findings.recommendation: Confirm whether CI provisions a real DATABASE_URL so these suites run un-skipped there; if the skip is environment-conditional, add a CI-only Postgres service (e.g. via GitHub Actions services:) so tenant isolation is verified on every PR, not just locally when a DB happens to be available.
findings.workaround: Tests exist and presumably pass when a DB is available; this is a coverage-execution gap, not a missing-test gap.

findings.title: Expense markPaid is authorised by companyProcedure, letting non-finance roles pay out expenses
findings.description: The markPaid mutation is documented as 'Only HR / super_admin can do this' but is guarded by companyProcedure, which only excludes the 'employee' and 'candidate' roles. Every other role, including recruiter (who has no finance function), can mark any approved expense as paid. There is no expenses:pay capability check. Combined with BIZ-001, a single department_manager can submit, approve, and pay one expense end-to-end.
findings.expectedBehavior: Only a payroll/finance capability holder (e.g. payroll_admin, hr_manager, super_admin) should close out an expense as paid, and the payer should differ from the approver/submitter.
findings.actualBehavior: companyProcedure permits department_manager, hr_specialist, payroll_admin and recruiter; the code comment does not match the guard.

evidence.snippet:
markPaid: companyProcedure
  .input(z.object({ id: z.string().uuid() }))
  .mutation(async ({ ctx, input }) => { ... status: "paid", paidAt: new Date() ... })

findings.reproduction: As a recruiter, call expense.markPaid { id } for any approved expense. It succeeds and sets status='paid'.
findings.technicalRisk: Financial state 'paid' reachable by roles outside finance; no capability gate.
findings.businessRisk: Loss of segregation of duties on cash-out; a single actor can complete the whole approve+pay chain, enabling reimbursement fraud.
findings.recommendation: Replace companyProcedure with requireCapability of a new 'expenses:pay' (grant only to payroll_admin/hr_manager/super_admin), and add a payer != approver != submitter check. Write an audit record on payout.
findings.workaround: Temporarily switch markPaid to requireRole('super_admin','hr_manager','payroll_admin').

findings.title: Duplicate final settlement / EOSB: no uniqueness or status guard on settlement creation
findings.description: settlement.create inserts a new final_settlements row on every call with no check for an existing settlement for the same employee, and the table has no unique constraint or 'status/paid' field. offboarding.initiate also inserts an independent final_settlements row. The same employee can therefore have multiple EOSB records, each a candidate for payout, with no idempotency or 'already settled' guard.
findings.expectedBehavior: An employee should have at most one active final settlement; a repeat attempt should update the existing record or be rejected, and a settled/paid record must be immutable.
findings.actualBehavior: Every create/initiate call inserts a fresh row; nothing prevents duplicates.

evidence.snippet: const [settlement] = await ctx.db.insert(schema.tenant.finalSettlements).values({ employeeId: input.employeeId, esbAmount: eosb.eosbAmount.toString(), ... }).returning();  // no existing-settlement check
const [settlement] = await ctx.db.insert(schema.tenant.finalSettlements).values({ employeeId: input.employeeId, exitReason: `${input.primaryReason} :: ...` }).returning();  // second independent row
export const finalSettlements = pgTable("final_settlements", { id: ..., employeeId: uuid(...).notNull()...  // no unique(employeeId), no status/paid column

findings.reproduction: Call settlement.create for employee X twice (or create then offboarding.initiate). Two final_settlements rows exist for X, each with an EOSB amount.
findings.technicalRisk: No idempotency on a high-value financial record.
findings.businessRisk: Duplicate end-of-service payouts (potentially tens of thousands of SAR each) if downstream payment reads more than one row.
findings.recommendation: Add a unique/active constraint on employeeId (or a settlement status), check for an existing settlement before insert, and make a paid settlement immutable. Consolidate offboarding.initiate to reuse/upsert the same row.
findings.workaround: HR procedure to check for an existing settlement before creating one.

findings.title: Real production Supabase DB connection string (with password) stored in cleartext file in repo working tree
findings.description: _db_url.txt at the repo root contains a live Supabase pooler connection string including the database password and the tenant search_path (rukn_energy_services). The file is listed in .gitignore (so it is not committed to git history that was checked), but it sits in the developer working tree in plaintext, is trivially shared via backups/zips/screen-shares, and grants direct SQL access to production PII (iqama, IBAN, salary). A credential of this sensitivity must not live in a loose text file.
findings.expectedBehavior: Production DB credentials live only in a secret manager / Vercel env vars, never in a working-tree file.
findings.actualBehavior: Full production connection string with password is readable from _db_url.txt.

evidence.snippet: postgresql://postgres.iefwhxxhrycaalhxkfgp:0uKN********@aws-1-ap-south-1.pooler.supabase.com:5432/postgres?options=-csearch_path%3Drukn_energy_services

findings.reproduction: Open _db_url.txt at repo root; the password is present in cleartext.
findings.technicalRisk: Anyone obtaining the working tree gains full read/write access to the production tenant database.
findings.businessRisk: Direct PDPL breach vector; unauthorized access to all tenant PII and payroll data.
findings.recommendation: Rotate the Supabase database password immediately, delete _db_url.txt from disk, and confirm it never entered git history (git log --all -- _db_url.txt). Store the URL only in the secret manager.
findings.workaround: Delete the file now; rotate the credential regardless.

findings.title: Password-reset token and user email written to server logs (account-takeover via log access)
findings.description: The request-reset handler logs the full reset URL (which embeds the raw single-use reset token) together with the user's email via console.info. Server logs are typically retained and broadly readable (Vercel log drains, observability tools). Anyone with log access can read the token within its 1-hour TTL and complete an account takeover; the email is also PII exposed in logs. The DB correctly stores only the token hash, so the log is the weak link.
findings.expectedBehavior: Reset links must be delivered only to the user's mailbox and never logged; at most log a non-sensitive event id.
findings.actualBehavior: console.info("[auth] password reset link generated for", email, resetUrl) logs email + raw token.

evidence.snippet: const resetUrl = `${base}/reset-password?email=${encodeURIComponent(email)}&token=${rawToken}`;
console.info("[auth] password reset link generated for", email, resetUrl);

findings.reproduction: Call POST /api/auth/request-reset with a registered email; inspect server logs for the reset URL containing token and email.
findings.technicalRisk: Log reader obtains a valid reset token and hijacks the account.
findings.businessRisk: Account takeover of HR/admin accounts; PDPL breach of PII in logs.
findings.recommendation: Wire the email delivery (TODO(email)) and remove the console.info entirely, or gate it behind NODE_ENV !== 'production' with the token redacted. Never log tokens or email.
findings.workaround: Immediately restrict log access and set short log retention until fixed.

findings.title: Unauthenticated proxy to Qiwa government API via /api/qiwa (re-opens SEC-009 through a REST twin)
findings.exploitability: moderate (requires QIWA_CLIENT_ID/SECRET configured to reach the government API)
findings.description: The custom route /api/qiwa (POST/PUT/GET) authenticates to the Qiwa government platform with the server's QIWA_CLIENT_ID/SECRET and forwards the caller's raw JSON body to POST/PUT/GET https://api.qiwa.sa/api/v2/employees. It performs NO session check, NO role check and NO tenant resolution. The tRPC twin qiwa.sync was correctly re-gated to requireRole(super_admin, hr_manager, payroll_admin) under SEC-009, but this REST endpoint is a wide-open parallel door to the same government actions (create / update / query employee records). It is also absent from the middleware matcher, so no rate-limit applies.
findings.expectedBehavior: Any government-filing/integration action must require an authenticated session with integrations/payroll authority (requireRole super_admin/hr_manager/payroll_admin), mirroring qiwa.sync. Prefer removing the REST route and routing the UI through the guarded tRPC procedure.
findings.actualBehavior: An anonymous request to /api/qiwa is proxied straight to the Qiwa government API using the tenant's credentials; if credentials are unset it returns 500 'Qiwa credentials not configured' (fail-open on authz, fail-closed only on missing creds).
findings.reproduction: With QIWA creds configured: curl -X POST https://<host>/api/qiwa -H 'content-type: application/json' -d '{...employee...}' (no cookie) -> forwarded to the Qiwa government platform.
findings.technicalRisk: Anonymous invocation of external government create/update/query actions using server-held credentials; abuse/SSRF-style relay of the tenant's Qiwa identity.
findings.businessRisk: Wrongful or unauthorized government labor-contract actions attributable to the tenant; regulatory exposure. Undermines the SEC-009 remediation.
findings.recommendation: Delete apps/web/app/api/qiwa/route.ts (its capability is fully covered by qiwa.sync), or add auth()+requireRole gating at the top of each handler and register the path in the middleware matcher.
findings.workaround: Block /api/qiwa at the edge and/or leave QIWA credentials unset until fixed.

findings.title: attendance.getSubtree fetches every employee in the tenant on every call instead of the requested org subtree
findings.page: Attendance guide map (org subtree with last-known GPS location)
findings.description: The comment above the query says "Recursive CTE to get all employees under rootEmployeeId" but the actual Drizzle call has no `where` clause and no `limit`, so it loads the entire employees table (with department join) for the tenant on every request, then filters/builds the org tree in application memory (apps/web/trpc/routers/attendance.ts:722-758). A second unbounded findMany over attendanceRecords (with: {}) then follows for every employee in that full result set.
findings.expectedBehavior: Only employees within the requested rootEmployeeId's subtree (and their latest attendance record) should be fetched from the database, ideally via a recursive CTE or a scoped WHERE clause.
findings.actualBehavior: ctx.db.query.employees.findMany({ with: { department: true } }) has no where/limit — it is a full tenant-wide employee scan on every guide-map request.
findings.reproduction: Read apps/web/trpc/routers/attendance.ts lines 710-780 (getSubtree procedure) — no where clause is ever applied to the employees.findMany call before the in-memory tree/filter logic runs.
findings.technicalRisk: Every guide-map page load/refresh does an O(all tenant employees) fetch + O(n) in-memory tree walk regardless of how small the requested subtree is; scales poorly for larger tenants and increases DB load under concurrent map views.
findings.businessRisk: Slower/laggy GPS map for larger customers; wasted DB read capacity that could throttle other tenants sharing the database.
findings.recommendation: Replace the full-table findMany with a recursive CTE (raw SQL via Drizzle's sql`` helper) or an iterative BFS that only expands children of rootEmployeeId, and add a limit as a safety cap.
findings.workaround: None; acceptable today only because demo/pilot tenants are small.

findings.title: Zero automated tests for the two largest business-logic routers: retention.ts (1611 lines) and recruitment.ts (812 lines)
findings.page: Retention (goals, reviews, skills, succession, engagement, rewards, compensation), Recruitment (jobs, candidates, applications, interviews, offers, onboarding, referrals, background/reference checks)
findings.description: The vitest run (152 tests passed across 26 suite files) contains no test file for apps/web/trpc/routers/retention.ts or apps/web/trpc/routers/recruitment.ts. These are the two single largest source files in the repo (1611 and 812 lines respectively) and implement RBAC-scoped CRUD + workflow logic for goals/OKRs, performance reviews, skills/succession, engagement surveys, rewards/compensation, and the full recruitment pipeline (candidates through onboarding). No __tests__ directory reference to `retention` or `recruitment` domain logic exists anywhere in packages/ or apps/web/src/__tests__.
findings.expectedBehavior: Business-logic routers of this size, especially ones gating access by requireCapability/requireRole (payroll:view_company, performance:view_team, etc.), should have integration tests covering at least the RBAC gate and core CRUD/workflow paths.
findings.actualBehavior: No test files reference these routers; coverage is 0% at the unit/integration level for both.

evidence.snippet: 1611 total lines; no corresponding packages/*/__tests__ or apps/web/src/__tests__ file exists for this router
812 total lines; no corresponding test file exists
Test Files  1 failed | 25 passed (26)  — no retention/recruitment suite among the 26

findings.reproduction: find . -name '*.test.ts' | grep -i 'retention\|recruitment' — no results.
findings.technicalRisk: Regressions in RBAC scoping (e.g. perfView/compView capability gates), pagination, or workflow state transitions in these two routers would not be caught by the test suite before reaching production.
findings.businessRisk: These modules touch performance ratings, succession planning, compensation, and the hiring pipeline — errors here are visible to managers and candidates and could leak data across the department/company boundary without a test catching it.
findings.recommendation: Add integration tests per sub-router (goal, reviewCycle, skill, succession, engagement, reward, compensation for retention; job/candidate/application/interview/offer/onboarding/referral for recruitment) covering: RBAC gate rejection for unauthorized roles, pagination correctness, and at least one full create->read->update happy path per entity.
findings.workaround: None.

findings.title: pnpm audit: drizzle-orm pinned below the patched version for a high-severity SQL-injection advisory
findings.description: `pnpm audit --audit-level=high` reports one high-severity advisory: "Drizzle ORM has SQL injection via improperly escaped SQL identifiers" (GHSA-gpj5-g38j-94v9), affecting drizzle-orm <0.45.2. packages/db/package.json pins "drizzle-orm": "^0.38.0" and the resolved version is 0.38.4 (12 dependency paths). `pnpm audit` (no level filter) additionally reports 4 moderate advisories: esbuild <=0.24.2 (dev-server request/response exposure, via drizzle-kit's esbuild-kit chain), next-auth 5.0.0-beta.25 (email misdelivery, patched at beta.30), and postcss <8.5.10 (XSS via unescaped </style> in stringify output, pulled in via Next.js 16.2.10).
findings.expectedBehavior: Dependencies with known high-severity advisories should be upgraded to patched versions promptly, especially the ORM used for every tenant-scoped query.
findings.actualBehavior: drizzle-orm is pinned to ^0.38.0 (resolves to 0.38.4), below the 0.45.2 patched version.

evidence.snippet: "drizzle-orm": "^0.38.0",
high | Drizzle ORM has SQL injection via improperly escaped SQL identifiers | Vulnerable versions <0.45.2 | Patched versions >=0.45.2 | GHSA-gpj5-g38j-94v9

findings.reproduction: cd hrms-app && pnpm audit --audit-level=high  (also: pnpm audit, for the 4 additional moderate findings)
findings.technicalRisk: If any code path builds SQL identifiers (table/column names) from less-trusted input while on the vulnerable drizzle-orm version, injection is possible; even if no such path currently exists, staying on a flagged version is a standing liability that must be re-checked with every new query added.
findings.businessRisk: A published CVE against the core data-access library of an HR/payroll SaaS handling PII and salary data is a due-diligence red flag for enterprise customers and auditors.
findings.recommendation: Upgrade drizzle-orm to >=0.45.2 (check for breaking changes against drizzle-kit and schema code), upgrade next-auth to >=5.0.0-beta.30, and let drizzle-kit pull in a patched esbuild-kit/esbuild chain; postcss will resolve once Next.js patches its bundled dependency.
findings.workaround: None; not yet exploited but exposure is real.

findings.title: Form labels not programmatically associated with inputs (missing htmlFor/id) across core HR forms
findings.role: HR Manager, Employee, Department Manager, Super Admin
findings.wcagCriterion: 1.3.1 Info and Relationships (A), 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A)
findings.description: Several of the app's oldest/core forms render plain `<label>` text with no `htmlFor`, paired with `<Input>`/`<select>` elements that have no `id`. This breaks the programmatic label-control relationship: clicking the label text does not focus the field, and screen readers announce the field with no accessible name (or fall back to the placeholder only). By contrast, newer forms (login, signup, recruitment/*, retention/*, offboarding/*, documents/upload) consistently pair `htmlFor` with matching `id`s, showing the pattern is known and used elsewhere in the codebase — it just wasn't applied to these forms.
findings.expectedBehavior: Every `<label>` should carry `htmlFor` matching the paired input's `id` (or wrap the input), so assistive technology announces a clear field name and label-click focuses the control — as already done correctly in apps/web/app/(auth)/login/login-form.tsx.
findings.actualBehavior: Labels and inputs are visually adjacent but have no id/htmlFor link in the forms listed in Evidence.

evidence.snippet:
<label className="text-sm font-medium text-slate-700">Full Name *</label>
<Input value={fullName} onChange={(e) => setFullName(e.target.value)} placeholder="Employee full name" />
{currentEmployee?.fullName ?? "Loading..."}

<label htmlFor="email" className="block text-sm font-medium text-slate-700">Email</label>
<input id="email" ... />

findings.reproduction: 1. Open /employees/new, step to the form. 2. Inspect any field's label in devtools — no `for` attribute; input has no `id`. 3. With a screen reader (NVDA/VoiceOver), tab into 'Full Name' — it is announced generically rather than as 'Full Name, edit text'.
findings.technicalRisk: Low complexity, high fan-out: the same unlabeled pattern repeats across ~9 fields per form in at least 11 files.
findings.businessRisk: Blocks or degrades task completion for screen-reader users (employees self-onboarding, HR staff with visual impairments) on some of the most business-critical forms in the product (new hire creation, leave requests, payroll setup).
findings.recommendation: Add matching `id`/`htmlFor` pairs to all fields in the listed forms (or introduce a small `<FormField>` wrapper component in packages/ui that auto-generates and wires ids, then migrate these pages to it) to prevent regression.
findings.workaround: Sighted mouse/touch users are unaffected; only assistive-technology users are impacted.

findings.title: No absolute session lifetime and stale role/privileges in JWT (no revocation on role change or deactivation)
findings.exploitability: moderate - requires prior compromise or a privilege change window
findings.description: The JWT session uses a 30-minute rolling idle window (maxAge 30m, updateAge 5m) with NO absolute cap, so an active session can be extended indefinitely. The jwt callback only re-hydrates employeeId on later requests - it never re-reads the user's role or verifies the account still exists/is enabled. Consequently a role downgrade, a role removal, or an account deletion is not reflected until the idle window lapses (up to 30 min), and there is no server-side revocation because the strategy is stateless JWT. The users table has no 'disabled/status' column, so an operator cannot force-logout or lock out an active token at all.
findings.expectedBehavior: Role/authorization changes and account deactivation should take effect on the next request (re-read role from DB in the jwt callback or check a token version / session revocation list), and sessions should have an absolute maximum lifetime.
findings.actualBehavior: Role is copied only at first sign-in; later requests keep the old role. No absolute expiry, no revocation, no account-disabled concept.

evidence.snippet: session: { strategy: 'jwt', maxAge: 30 * 60, updateAge: 5 * 60 },

findings.reproduction: Sign in as a user, have an admin change that user's role to a lower one; the active session keeps admin capabilities until the 30-min idle window expires. Deleting the user does not invalidate the live token.
findings.technicalRisk: Privilege changes and deprovisioning are not enforced in real time; a stolen JWT cannot be revoked.
findings.businessRisk: Terminated employees retain access for the idle window; least-privilege violations during role transitions.
findings.recommendation: Re-read role (and an is_active/token_version) from the DB in the jwt callback on each request, add an absolute session cap, and add an account-status column so operators can force revocation.
findings.workaround: Reduce maxAge; instruct admins that role changes take effect after the idle window.

findings.title: Account lockout silently disabled by schema drift - lockout columns absent from Drizzle schema
findings.description: The durable account-lockout code reads/writes failed_login_attempts and locked_until via raw SQL wrapped in try/catch that swallows all errors and 'disables lockout until migrated' when the columns are missing. Those columns are defined ONLY in migration 0008; they are NOT present in the Drizzle schema (users.ts). Because CLAUDE.md documents `pnpm db:push` (schema-push) as a workflow, a push from the drifted schema can DROP failed_login_attempts/locked_until, silently turning brute-force lockout off with no error surfaced. Prior memory notes already flag attendance schema drift in this repo.
findings.expectedBehavior: Lockout columns should be part of the source-of-truth Drizzle schema so db:push preserves them, and a missing-column condition should be logged loudly, not swallowed.
findings.actualBehavior: Columns live only in a hand-written migration; the schema object omits them; failures are swallowed and lockout becomes a no-op.

evidence.snippet:
mfaSecret: text("mfa_secret"),
  employeeId: uuid("employee_id"),
  createdAt: timestamp("created_at").defaultNow().notNull(),

ALTER TABLE public.users ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS failed_login_attempts INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
ALTER TABLE public.users ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS locked_until TIMESTAMP;

findings.reproduction: Run pnpm db:push from the current schema (which lacks the columns) against a DB that has them -> Drizzle proposes dropping failed_login_attempts/locked_until; after that, recordFailedAttempt/getLockState throw and are swallowed, so accounts never lock.
findings.technicalRisk: Silent loss of the primary brute-force defense on the NextAuth login path.
findings.businessRisk: Undetected regression enabling password guessing at scale.
findings.recommendation: Add failedLoginAttempts and lockedUntil to the Drizzle users schema so they are managed and preserved; replace the silent catch with a one-time warning log/metric.
findings.workaround: Use db:migrate (not db:push) in all environments; verify columns exist post-deploy.

findings.title: No audit trail on any expense mutation (create / approve / markPaid)
findings.description: The audit helper writeAudit is used for payroll runs, reopens and settlements, but the expense router never calls it. Approvals and payouts of company money leave no who/when/old/new record, so the self-approval and over-broad-payout risks (BIZ-001/002) cannot be detected or investigated after the fact.
findings.expectedBehavior: Every financial state change (approve, reject, pay) should write an audit_logs entry with actor, role, old/new status and amount, per the project's 'Audit log for all sensitive operations' rule.
findings.actualBehavior: No writeAudit calls exist anywhere in expense.ts; the module is entirely un-audited.

evidence.snippet: export async function writeAudit(ctx: AuditableContext, entry: AuditEntry): Promise<void> { ... }

findings.reproduction: Approve and mark an expense paid, then inspect audit_logs: no rows are written for the action.
findings.technicalRisk: No non-repudiation for expense financial events.
findings.businessRisk: Fraud and error are undetectable; fails internal-control and audit expectations for a payroll product.
findings.recommendation: Add writeAudit to approve, reject and markPaid (and ideally create/cancel), recording actor, role, amount and status transition.
findings.workaround: None at the application layer; rely on DB-level change data capture if available.

findings.title: All timestamps use `timestamp` (without time zone) — Riyadh (UTC+3) correctness risk for punch/approval/payroll times
findings.description: The generated migration 0000 contains zero timestamptz columns; every createdAt/updatedAt and event timestamp (punch_in_at, punch_out_at, approved_at, paid_at, completed_at, sent_at, resolved_at, etc.) is `timestamp` without time zone. Serverless writers run in UTC while the business operates in Asia/Riyadh (UTC+3). Naive timestamps store whatever wall clock the writer used with no offset, so a punch recorded in UTC and displayed as Riyadh local (or vice versa) can be off by 3 hours — material for attendance late/early minutes, overtime, and payroll period boundaries. (Pure `date` columns for hire/termination/leave/period_month are acceptable.) Note inconsistency: hand-written migration 0002 uses timestamptz, so the codebase is mixed.
findings.expectedBehavior: Instant-in-time columns should be timestamptz (stored as UTC, rendered in Asia/Riyadh), consistently across schema and migrations.
findings.actualBehavior: timestamp without time zone everywhere in the Drizzle schema; timestamptz only in one ad-hoc migration.

evidence.snippet: "completed_at" timestamp, "created_at" timestamp DEFAULT now() NOT NULL, "updated_at" timestamp DEFAULT now() NOT NULL   -- payroll_runs; grep timestamptz -> 0 matches
punchInAt: timestamp('punch_in_at'), punchOutAt: timestamp('punch_out_at'),
created_at timestamptz DEFAULT now() NOT NULL, updated_at timestamptz DEFAULT now() NOT NULL   -- the only timestamptz use

findings.reproduction: grep -c timestamptz packages/db/drizzle/0000_thin_freak.sql -> 0. Record a punch from a UTC serverless function, render in Riyadh -> 3h skew.
findings.technicalRisk: Off-by-offset in attendance minutes, overtime, and payroll period cutoffs; ambiguous ordering across DST-free but offset boundaries.
findings.businessRisk: Incorrect late/overtime and payroll amounts; disputes; compliance exposure on working-hours records.
findings.recommendation: Migrate instant columns to timestamptz and standardize on UTC storage + Asia/Riyadh presentation. If mass migration is too costly short-term, enforce a single writer timezone (UTC) app-wide and document it; long-term convert to timestamptz.
findings.workaround: Ensure every writer sets time in UTC and every reader converts to Asia/Riyadh explicitly.

findings.title: Field-encryption key falls back to a public hardcoded dev key outside NODE_ENV=production; no key rotation/versioning
findings.exploitability: Any non-production environment (preview/staging) missing FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY encrypts PII under a key committed to the repo — trivially decryptable by anyone with the source.
findings.description: deriveKeys() throws only when NODE_ENV === 'production'. In any other environment (preview, staging, CI, or a misconfigured prod where NODE_ENV isn't exactly 'production'), a missing FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY silently uses DEV_FALLBACK_KEY = 'hrms-dev-only-insecure-field-encryption-key', which is in source control. PII (iqama/passport/IBAN) written in such environments is effectively plaintext. Separately, the construction has no key-versioning/rotation: the marker is a static 'encv1:' and the key label is fixed 'v1'; rotating FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY changes every deterministic ciphertext and makes all previously stored values fail GCM auth on read (decryptField throws), with no dual-key/rewrap path.
findings.expectedBehavior: Missing key should fail closed in every deployed environment (not just production). A key id should be embedded so keys can be rotated with a rewrap/dual-read window.
findings.actualBehavior: Silent dev-key fallback for all non-production NODE_ENV; no rotation mechanism.

evidence.snippet: const MARKER = 'encv1:'; ... const DEV_FALLBACK_KEY = 'hrms-dev-only-insecure-field-encryption-key';

findings.reproduction: Deploy a preview build with NODE_ENV!=='production' and no FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY; write an employee iqama; read the raw column -> ciphertext decryptable with the repo's DEV_FALLBACK_KEY. Rotate the key -> existing rows throw on read.
findings.technicalRisk: PII confidentiality lost in non-prod; no safe key rotation; a bad key rollover bricks reads of all encrypted PII.
findings.businessRisk: PDPL/PII exposure in staging/preview; operational risk if the key is ever rotated or lost.
findings.recommendation: Fail closed whenever the app is deployed (e.g. require the key unless an explicit local/test flag is set), never fall back to a committed key in any hosted environment. Add a key id to the marker (encv1:<keyid>:) and implement dual-read (old+new key) + background rewrap for rotation. Validate presence at startup via packages/config env.
findings.workaround: Set FIELD_ENCRYPTION_KEY in every deployed environment including preview/staging.
findings.estimatedEffort: M (fail-closed is S; rotation support is M)

findings.title: Single flat Drizzle migration mixes public and all tenant tables into one unqualified schema; no per-tenant migration runner
findings.exploitability: Operator running db:migrate/db:push against the schema-per-tenant DB materializes all tenant tables in public, cross-contaminating the isolation model.
findings.description: drizzle.config.ts scans schema/**/*.ts (both public and tenant). The generated 0000_thin_freak.sql contains 78 CREATE TABLE statements — public tables (accounts, sessions, users, tenants, verification_tokens, invite_token_index) AND all tenant tables (employees, payslips, etc.) — with no schema qualifier and 106 ADD CONSTRAINT FKs, all in a single schema. drizzle-kit migrate/push targets the connection's default schema (public), so running it would create every tenant table in public. There is no per-tenant migration runner; ongoing schema evolution for tenants is done via ad-hoc DO-block or hardcoded migrations (DB-002), which is the root cause of the drift findings above.
findings.expectedBehavior: A clear separation: public-schema migrations for shared tables, and a dedicated per-tenant migration/DDL pipeline replaying an authoritative bundle into each tenant_<hex> schema. drizzle-kit generate should not blend the two.
findings.actualBehavior: One flat migration blends public + tenant tables; no mechanism keeps existing tenant schemas in sync with the Drizzle schema.

evidence.snippet: schema: './src/schema/**/*.ts', out: './drizzle', dialect: 'postgresql', dbCredentials: { url: process.env.DATABASE_URL ... }
CREATE TABLE "accounts" ...; CREATE TABLE "employees" ...; CREATE TABLE "payslips" ...; CREATE TABLE "tenants" ...; CREATE TABLE "users" ...   -- public + tenant mixed, unqualified
"db:push": "drizzle-kit push", "db:generate": "drizzle-kit generate", "db:migrate": "drizzle-kit migrate"

findings.reproduction: grep 'CREATE TABLE' 0000_thin_freak.sql -> 78 tables incl. both public and tenant. Run drizzle-kit migrate against the dev DB -> tenant tables land in public.
findings.technicalRisk: Isolation model violated if migrate/push is run; no reproducible way to evolve tenant schemas -> perpetual drift (DB-001..005).
findings.businessRisk: Operational fragility; risk of accidental cross-tenant table creation in public; hard to onboard/maintain tenants at scale.
findings.recommendation: Split schema generation: keep drizzle migrations for public-schema tables only, and build a first-class per-tenant migration runner (ordered SQL bundle applied with search_path per tenant, tracked in a per-tenant migrations table). Regenerate provisioning DDL from the tenant schema. Remove tenant tables from the public-targeted migration path.
findings.workaround: Never run db:migrate/db:push against the shared DB without a schema target; manage tenant DDL via scripts.

findings.title: users.mfa_secret (TOTP seed) is stored plaintext, not wrapped in encryptedText
findings.exploitability: A read of the users table (SQLi, backup leak, DB access) yields TOTP seeds, letting an attacker generate valid second factors and defeat MFA.
findings.description: The users table stores mfaSecret as plain text. Unlike employee iqama/passport/IBAN, the MFA shared secret is not wrapped in the encryptedText codec, so the TOTP seed sits at rest in cleartext. Anyone able to read the row can reconstruct the user's one-time codes.
findings.expectedBehavior: MFA/TOTP secrets should be encrypted at rest (or stored in a dedicated secret store), consistent with the SEC-008 encryption applied to other PII.
findings.actualBehavior: mfa_secret is plaintext text.

evidence.snippet: mfaSecret: text('mfa_secret'),
CREATE TABLE "users" ( ... "mfa_secret" text, ... )   -- no encryption

findings.reproduction: Read users.mfa_secret -> base32 TOTP seed in cleartext; feed to any TOTP generator to produce valid codes.
findings.technicalRisk: MFA bypass if the users table is exposed.
findings.businessRisk: Account takeover risk; weakens the MFA control the product advertises.
findings.recommendation: Wrap mfa_secret with the encryptedText codec (or a KMS-backed secret store). Since MFA secrets need no equality/uniqueness, prefer a randomized-IV variant rather than the deterministic codec to avoid equality leakage. Backfill existing rows.
findings.workaround: Restrict DB read access; rotate MFA enrollments if exposure suspected.

findings.title: Audit log omits authentication, role-assignment, export, and PII-view events
findings.description: writeAudit is invoked only for employee create/update/delete, payroll runs, settlement, and PDPL self-export. Security- and privacy-relevant events are NOT persisted to audit_logs: successful/failed logins and lockouts (only ephemeral console.warn), role assignment/changes via invites, MFA enable/disable, document/letter generation (PRIV-010), and PII/salary views. PDPL accountability and standard security monitoring expect durable auth and access-to-PII trails.
findings.expectedBehavior: Login/logout, lockout, role changes, MFA changes, config changes, and PII/export access are written to a durable audit trail with actor/action/target/timestamp.
findings.actualBehavior: Auth events are only console-logged; role/MFA/export/view events are not audited.

evidence.snippet: export const auditRouter = createTRPCRouter({ list: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager") ... })
console.warn('[auth] failed login attempt for', email);  // not persisted to audit_logs
role: role ?? "employee",  // role assignment, no writeAudit

findings.reproduction: Log in, assign a role via invite, generate a letter; query audit_logs — none of these appear.
findings.technicalRisk: No forensic trail for authentication abuse, privilege changes, or PII access.
findings.businessRisk: PDPL accountability gap; incident investigation and breach reporting hampered.
findings.recommendation: Emit audit_logs entries for auth (login/logout/lockout/MFA), role/permission changes, config changes, and PII exports/views. Keep the existing tenant-scoped, read-only audit read surface.
findings.workaround: Ship auth events to a durable log store as an interim measure.

findings.title: employee.getById / employee.list expose salary + payslips to roles lacking payroll:view_company; getById is not department-scoped
findings.description: employee.list and employee.getById are companyProcedure (any staff role). The employees row carries plaintext salaryBasic/salaryHousing/salaryTransport columns and no column projection is applied, so recruiter and department_manager — who hold people:view_company but NOT payroll:view_company — read every employee's base salary. Worse, employee.getById additionally loads the payslips relation (full pay history) and, unlike employee.list, applies NO department scoping. A department_manager can therefore pass any employeeId to getById and read that employee's complete record plus payslip financials, defeating the SEC-013 department-scope intent for reads. The prior audit's permission matrix marked employee.list/getById as 'Secure', missing this.
findings.expectedBehavior: Salary columns and the payslips relation should be gated to PAYROLL_VIEW_ROLES / payroll:view_company (projected out for other roles), and employee.getById should apply the same getManagedDepartmentIds scoping as employee.list for department_manager.
findings.actualBehavior: recruiter and department_manager receive salaryBasic/Housing/Transport for all employees via list; department_manager receives any employee's full record + payslips via getById with no scope check.

evidence.snippet:
