salaryBasic:     numeric("salary_basic",     { precision: 12, scale: 2 }).notNull(),
  salaryHousing:   numeric("salary_housing",   { precision: 12, scale: 2 }).notNull().default("0"),
  salaryTransport: numeric("salary_transport", { precision: 12, scale: 2 }).notNull().default("0"),

findings.reproduction: Login as recruiter -> call employee.list -> response rows include salaryBasic/salaryHousing/salaryTransport. Login as department_manager -> call employee.getById with an employeeId outside your department -> returns that employee's full record + payslips.
findings.technicalRisk: Within-tenant salary and pay-history confidentiality breach; department-scope bypass on the getById read path.
findings.businessRisk: Staff without compensation authority learn colleagues' salaries; contradicts the capability model and SEC-013 scoping the audit claims complete.
findings.recommendation: Add a column projection to employee.list/getById that omits salary fields for roles without payroll:view_company, gate the payslips relation on PAYROLL_VIEW_ROLES, and apply getManagedDepartmentIds scoping inside getById for department_manager.
findings.workaround: None client-side; server change required.

findings.title: Required-field markers are visual-only and validation errors are not announced to assistive technology
findings.description: Required fields are marked with a plain "*" in the label text, but the underlying ``/`
findings.expectedBehavior: Required inputs should carry `required`/`aria-required="true"`; the error summary should be in a `role="alert"` (or `aria-live="assertive"`) container so screen-reader users are told immediately when submission fails, matching apps/web/app/(auth)/login/login-form.tsx:78-85.
findings.actualBehavior: Submitting either form with missing/invalid data updates a plain div with the error text; a screen-reader user who has already moved focus away from the form is not notified anything happened.

evidence.snippet:
{error &&

{error}

}

findings.reproduction: 1. Open /leave/new, submit with no dates selected. 2. Error text appears visually but a screen reader user focused elsewhere gets no announcement.
findings.technicalRisk: Low — add `role="alert"` to the existing conditional div/p, and `required` to inputs already tracked as mandatory in the submit handler's validation.
findings.businessRisk: Screen-reader users may repeatedly submit a failing form without understanding why, especially self-service employees filing leave requests unassisted.
findings.recommendation: Add `role="alert"` to error containers and `required`/`aria-required` to fields already validated as mandatory in handleSubmit.
findings.workaround: Sighted users see the red banner directly below the button they just clicked.

findings.title: pnpm lint fails with 372 errors (almost entirely no-explicit-any / no-non-null-assertion) confined to the web app
findings.description: `pnpm lint` fails (turbo reports @hrms-app/web#lint exited 1). All 16 other packages (auth, config, date, db, demo, documents, email, leave, llm, payroll, qiwa, queue, ui, validators, etc.) lint clean. Only apps/web fails, with 387 problems (372 errors, 15 warnings) across ~84 files. The dominant rule is @typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any (the large majority of the 372 errors — 295 raw `: any`/`as any` occurrences were found across apps/web by static grep), followed by @typescript-eslint/no-non-null-assertion (~30+ occurrences, e.g. attendance/me/page.tsx, payroll pages, leave.ts, invite.ts) and a smaller number of no-unused-vars.
findings.expectedBehavior: `pnpm lint` should exit 0 across all packages, per the project's own ESLint config which forbids `any` and non-null assertions.
findings.actualBehavior: @hrms-app/web#lint exits with code 1; 372 errors reported.

evidence.snippet: orderBy: (shifts: any, { asc }: any) => asc(shifts.name),
(non-null assertion flagged by @typescript-eslint/no-non-null-assertion)
@hrms-app/web:lint: ✖ 387 problems (372 errors, 15 warnings)

findings.reproduction: cd hrms-app && pnpm lint
findings.technicalRisk: Widespread `any` erases type safety exactly where drizzle-orm's inferred query/relation types would otherwise catch schema-mismatch bugs at compile time; non-null assertions (`!`) can throw at runtime if the assumption is ever wrong.
findings.businessRisk: A failing lint gate on every non-trivial page means CI/lint is effectively not enforced for apps/web today, letting type-safety erosion compound silently.
findings.recommendation: Triage by rule: replace `any` in router callback signatures (e.g. attendance.ts:60-61, department.ts:11, leave.ts:82) with the inferred Drizzle relation types; replace non-null assertions with explicit null checks or narrow the type upstream. Given the volume, tackle incrementally per-router rather than one large PR.
findings.workaround: None; lint is currently red.

findings.title: File upload storage path traversal via unsanitized category field
findings.description: In apps/web/app/api/upload/route.ts the object-storage key is built as `${tenantId}/${category}/${timestamp}_${safeFileName}`. The file name is sanitized (safeFileName), but the client-supplied `category` string is used raw. A value such as `../<otherTenantId>` would resolve the stored object outside the caller's tenant prefix within the Supabase bucket, breaking tenant isolation of stored files.
findings.expectedBehavior: The storage path segment derived from category must be validated against an allowlist (or sanitized the same way the filename is) so it cannot contain path separators or traversal sequences.
findings.actualBehavior: category is concatenated into the storage path without sanitization; `../` or slash characters allow escaping the tenant folder.
findings.reproduction: POST multipart to /api/upload with field category set to `../<victimTenantId>/docs` and observe the object written under the victim tenant's prefix.
findings.technicalRisk: Cross-tenant file placement/overwrite (x-upsert:true is set); breaks tenant storage isolation.
findings.businessRisk: Tenant data-isolation violation for uploaded documents; potential overwrite of another tenant's stored files.
findings.recommendation: Validate category against a fixed enum (e.g. policies, contracts, ids) or apply the same [^a-zA-Z0-9._-] sanitization used for the filename; reject null/empty category.
findings.workaround: Enforce an allowlist of category values server-side.

findings.title: MFA is opt-in only and not enforced by any policy (privileged roles can operate without MFA)
findings.description: TOTP MFA is only enforced when the individual user has already enrolled a secret (if (user.mfaSecret)). There is no organization policy requiring MFA for high-privilege roles (super_admin, hr_manager, payroll_admin), and nothing in signup/onboarding drives enrollment. A compromised password alone is sufficient for full admin access on any account that has not voluntarily enrolled.
findings.expectedBehavior: Privileged roles should be required to enrol MFA (block or step-up until enrolled), configurable per tenant.
findings.actualBehavior: MFA gate is skipped entirely for any account without mfaSecret; no role-based enforcement exists.
findings.reproduction: Create/sign in as an hr_manager without enrolling MFA; login succeeds with password only.
findings.technicalRisk: Password-only compromise yields full admin access to HR/payroll data.
findings.businessRisk: Elevated ATO risk for the highest-value accounts.
findings.recommendation: Add a tenant policy that requires MFA for privileged roles; block access (or force enrollment) until a secret is set.
findings.workaround: Operationally require admins to enrol MFA.

findings.title: In-memory middleware rate limiting is per-instance and ineffective on serverless/multi-instance
findings.description: Rate limiting is backed by a module-level in-memory Map. On Vercel/serverless (and any horizontally-scaled deploy) each instance has its own map and the map resets on cold start, so the 5/min login limit and other limits are enforced only per-instance - an attacker rotating across instances or after cold starts largely evades them. The durable per-account lockout mitigates this for the NextAuth path, but the standalone /api/auth/login route (AUTH-002) has neither. Redis (Upstash) is already configured in env but not used for rate limiting.
findings.expectedBehavior: Rate limiting for auth endpoints should use a shared store (Redis/Upstash) so limits are global across instances.
findings.actualBehavior: Limits use a per-process Map that resets on cold start.
findings.reproduction: Send login attempts spread across cold starts / instances; aggregate throughput exceeds the intended 5/min.
findings.technicalRisk: Weakened front-line brute-force throttle.
findings.businessRisk: Higher ATO risk under distributed attack.
findings.recommendation: Move auth rate limiting to Upstash Redis (already in env) keyed by IP and by account; keep the durable DB lockout as defense-in-depth.
findings.workaround: Add a WAF/edge rate limit in front of /api/auth/*.

findings.title: Leave balance can be over-drawn: balance is checked at submission but not re-validated at approval
findings.description: The available-balance check runs only in create, against the balance at submission time. Two (or more) non-overlapping requests can each pass because the balance is not decremented until approval. At approval, adjustLeaveBalance decrements with Math.max(0, ...), which silently floors a negative balance to 0 instead of rejecting, so the employee is granted more paid leave than their entitlement.
findings.expectedBehavior: Approval should re-validate the current balance and refuse (or partially grant) when the request exceeds the remaining balance; balance must never be allowed to go negative silently.
findings.actualBehavior: No balance re-check at approval; decrement clamps to 0, hiding the over-draw.

evidence.snippet:
if (bal && requestedDays > parseNumeric(bal.balance)) {
  throw new TRPCError({ code: "BAD_REQUEST", ... }); }  // only in create
const next = Math.max(0, parseNumeric(bal.balance) + delta);  // floors negative to 0, hides over-draw

findings.reproduction: With balance = 10 days, create request A (7 days) and request B (7 days, non-overlapping) — both pass the create check. Approve both. Final balance shows 0 instead of -4; employee took 14 of 10 days.
findings.technicalRisk: Balance integrity is not conserved across the request lifecycle.
findings.businessRisk: Unearned paid leave and inflated accrued-leave-payout liability at settlement.
findings.recommendation: Move/duplicate the balance check into updateStatus at the approval boundary (reject if days > current balance) and remove the silent Math.max floor (or log when a floor would occur).
findings.workaround: HR manually reconciles balances before approving stacked requests.

findings.title: Leave approvals have no segregation of duties: an approver can approve their own leave
findings.description: updateStatus authorises super_admin, hr_manager and department_manager to approve leave. There is no check that the approver is not the leave's own employee. A department_manager who heads their own department (dept scope check passes because their own department is in the managed set) can approve their own leave request; hr_manager/super_admin can approve their own outright.
findings.expectedBehavior: The approver of a leave request must not be the requesting employee; self-approval should be blocked and routed to a higher authority.
findings.actualBehavior: Only role and (for department_manager) department scope are checked; actor != subject is not.

evidence.snippet: updateStatus: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager", "department_manager") ...
if (ctx.user.role === "department_manager") { ... if (!deptId || !managed.includes(deptId)) throw FORBIDDEN }  // no actor != employee check

findings.reproduction: As a department_manager whose own employee record sits in a department they head, create a leave request (bound to self) then call updateStatus { id, data:{ status:'approved' } }. It succeeds.
findings.technicalRisk: Approval control is role-based only, not identity-aware.
findings.businessRisk: Managers self-grant paid leave; weakens leave governance and downstream accrual/payout accuracy.
findings.recommendation: In updateStatus, load the request's employeeId and reject when it equals the caller's employeeId (for the approval transition); require escalation to a higher approver.
findings.workaround: Policy control + monitor approvedByUserId vs employee.

findings.title: No data-subject rectification / erasure / consent-withdrawal endpoints (PDPL rights incomplete)
findings.description: A PDPL data-subject access (export) right is implemented (employee.exportMyData, audited). However there is no self-service or documented workflow for the right to correction, the right to destruction/erasure, or withdrawal of consent. Employee deletion is soft-delete only (status=terminated) which by design retains all PII indefinitely, and no anonymisation/retention-expiry job exists. Saudi PDPL grants data subjects rights to access, correction, and destruction; only access is covered in code.
findings.expectedBehavior: Provide (or document a controlled process for) correction, erasure/anonymisation, consent withdrawal, and a defined retention/disposal schedule.
findings.actualBehavior: Only exportMyData exists; soft-delete retains PII with no erasure/anonymisation path.

evidence.snippet: exportMyData: protectedProcedure.query(async ({ ctx }) => {  // PDPL access request
// Soft-delete only ... Hard deletion is intentionally not exposed.

findings.reproduction: Search routers for erasure/rectification/consent-withdrawal procedures; none exist.
findings.technicalRisk: Cannot fulfil a data-subject destruction/correction request within code.
findings.businessRisk: PDPL non-compliance exposure; regulator/complaint risk.
findings.recommendation: Add correction and erasure/anonymisation workflows (audited), a consent-withdrawal path, and a documented retention & disposal policy. Legal review required to define lawful retention periods (payroll/tax records vs erasable PII).
findings.workaround: Document a manual, audited DSR handling process in the interim.

findings.title: Salary certificate / experience letter generation exposes decrypted iqama + full salary and is not audited
findings.description: document.generateLetter builds a salary certificate / experience letter containing the employee's decrypted national ID (iqama) and full basic/housing/transport salary. This is a sensitive PII/financial export, but no writeAudit call is made, so there is no record of who generated a document containing another person's iqama and salary or when. writeAudit is used for employee/payroll/settlement mutations but not for this read/export.
findings.expectedBehavior: Every generation/export of a document containing another person's PII/salary is audited (actor, subject, type, timestamp).
findings.actualBehavior: generateLetter returns HTML with iqama + salary and writes no audit entry.

evidence.snippet: nationalId: employee.iqamaNumberEnc,
basicSalary: Number(employee.salaryBasic),
return { html, type: input.type };  // no writeAudit(...)

findings.reproduction: Call document.generateLetter; check audit_logs — no entry recorded for the export.
findings.technicalRisk: PII/salary disclosure with no attributable trail.
findings.businessRisk: PDPL accountability gap; cannot investigate misuse of salary certificates.
findings.recommendation: Add writeAudit('document.generate', entityType 'employee', subject id, {type}) to generateLetter; consider the same for any PII-bearing read/export.
findings.workaround: N/A

findings.title: leave.runAccrual issues one sequential DB write per employee×leaveType combination instead of a bulk operation
findings.description: The `runAccrual` mutation computes accrual results for every active employee × leave type in memory, then loops over the results issuing one `await ctx.db.insert(...)` or `await ctx.db.update(...)` per row sequentially inside a for-loop, rather than batching the writes.
findings.expectedBehavior: Bulk insert/update (e.g. a single multi-row insert with onConflict upsert, or Promise.all with a bounded concurrency) so the accrual run scales sub-linearly with employee count.
findings.actualBehavior: for (const result of results) { await ctx.db.insert(...) } / await ctx.db.update(...) — strictly sequential, one round trip per employee per leave type.
findings.reproduction: Read apps/web/trpc/routers/leave.ts lines 319-386 (runAccrual mutation).
findings.technicalRisk: For a tenant with N active employees and M leave types, this is up to N×M sequential DB round trips inside one HTTP-triggered mutation; risk of request timeout or long-held DB connections during monthly/annual cron runs as tenants grow.
findings.businessRisk: Cron job could start timing out or slowing down as customer headcount grows, silently delaying leave balance updates.
findings.recommendation: Batch inserts with a single multi-row INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, or chunk the loop with Promise.all in small batches (e.g. 20 at a time) to reduce round trips while bounding connection usage.
findings.workaround: Fine at current demo/pilot scale.

findings.title: No timeout or retry on any external HTTP call (Qiwa gov API, Gemini, Claude)
findings.page: AI assistant chat, Qiwa sync features
findings.description: Every external fetch() call in the Qiwa client (packages/qiwa/src/index.ts, 8 call sites: auth token, employees, contracts, contract status, health check) and both LLM providers (packages/llm/src/providers/gemini.ts:65, packages/llm/src/providers/claude.ts:56) is a bare `await fetch(...)` with no AbortController/signal timeout and no retry/backoff logic.
findings.expectedBehavior: External calls to third-party/government APIs and LLM providers should have a bounded timeout (e.g. 10-30s via AbortController) and at least one retry for transient network errors, so a slow upstream cannot hang a user-facing request indefinitely.
findings.actualBehavior: fetch() calls have no `signal`/timeout option anywhere in the qiwa or llm packages; a hung upstream connection will hang the calling tRPC procedure until the platform's own request timeout (if any) kicks in.

evidence.snippet: response = await fetch(url, { method: "POST", headers: {...}, body: JSON.stringify({...}) });
response = await fetch(ANTHROPIC_API_URL, { ... });
const response = await fetch(`${this.config.baseUrl}/api/v2/auth/token`, { ... });

findings.reproduction: grep -n "fetch(\|timeout\|AbortController\|retry" packages/qiwa/src/index.ts packages/llm/src/providers/gemini.ts packages/llm/src/providers/claude.ts — no timeout/retry keyword matches at any call site.
findings.technicalRisk: A slow or hanging Qiwa/Gemini/Claude endpoint ties up a server-side request (and DB connection, if the procedure holds one) for however long the runtime allows before killing it; no graceful degradation for transient failures.
findings.businessRisk: User-facing AI chat or Qiwa sync actions could appear to hang rather than fail fast with a retry or clear error, hurting perceived reliability during demos and real usage.
findings.recommendation: Add an AbortController-based timeout (e.g. 15s for LLM, 20s for Qiwa) to every external fetch, and a single retry with backoff for network-level failures (not for 4xx application errors).
findings.workaround: The AI chat path already has a try/catch fallback to a stub reply for context-fetching, which limits blast radius for that one call; the main completion call itself has no fallback.

findings.title: Several tRPC routers with real business logic have no dedicated unit/integration tests (attendance, expense, compliance, department, settlement, notification)
findings.module: Attendance / Expense / Compliance / Department / Settlement / Notification
findings.page: Attendance, Expenses, Compliance, Departments, Settlements, Notifications
findings.description: apps/web/trpc/routers/attendance.ts (788 lines: shifts, shift assignments, punches, exceptions, monthly reports, GPS subtree) has no matching unit/integration test file; it is only touched indirectly by two Playwright UI specs (attendance-guide-map.spec.ts, attendance-reports.spec.ts), which test rendering, not the underlying query/business logic (overtime calc, late/absence classification, subtree building). expense.ts, compliance.ts, department.ts, settlement.ts, and notification.ts routers similarly have no dedicated test file.
findings.expectedBehavior: Routers implementing calculations (overtime, late minutes) or RBAC-scoped visibility rules (expense.ts's SEC-004 remediation for pending-approval visibility) should have logic-level tests independent of the UI.
findings.actualBehavior: Only e2e/UI-level coverage exists for attendance; no coverage at all for expense/compliance/department/settlement/notification routers.

evidence.snippet: 788 lines; no packages/*/__tests__ or apps/web/src/__tests__ file references attendance business logic
// SESSION employee, never a client-supplied id (SEC-004 — was an IDOR). — this RBAC-sensitive logic has no regression test

findings.reproduction: find . -name '*.test.ts' | grep -iE 'attendance|expense|compliance|department|settlement|notification' — no matches beyond leave/payroll's own attendance-adjacent overtime tests in packages/payroll.
findings.technicalRisk: Regressions in overtime/late calculation or RBAC-scoped expense visibility (previously an IDOR, per the code comment) would only be caught by manual QA or production incidents, not CI.
findings.businessRisk: Payroll-adjacent overtime numbers and expense approval visibility are financially and legally sensitive (Saudi labour law); untested logic here carries compliance risk.
findings.recommendation: Prioritize a test for expense.ts's role-scoped visibility (given it references a prior IDOR fix) and attendance.ts's overtime/late calculation before adding new features to these routers.
findings.workaround: None.

findings.title: Incomplete SEC-006 fix: attendance company reads and leave company reads remain companyProcedure (recruiter over-exposure)
findings.url: attendance.list, attendance.monthlyReport, attendance.exceptions, attendance.assignment.list, leave.request.list, leave.balance.list
findings.description: The Phase 2 SEC-006 remediation applied requireCapability to recruitment, retention, settlement, document and ai reads, but did NOT touch the attendance and leave company-wide reads. attendance.list/monthlyReport/exceptions/assignment.list and leave.request.list/balance.list are still companyProcedure, which authorizes any non-employee/candidate staff role. The capability model grants attendance:view_company and leave:view_company to super_admin/hr_manager/hr_specialist/payroll_admin/department_manager but NOT recruiter. A recruiter therefore reads all-employee attendance (attendance.list embeds each employee row and last workLocation) and all-employee leave, outside their capability set. The 03-permission-matrix.md itself flagged these as 'Over-permissive (recruiter)' — they were never closed.
findings.expectedBehavior: attendance.list/monthlyReport/exceptions/assignment.list should be requireCapability('attendance:view_company') and leave.request.list/balance.list requireCapability('leave:view_company'), consistent with the rest of Phase 2.
findings.actualBehavior: recruiter (no attendance/leave company capability) can read every employee's attendance and leave records.

evidence.snippet:
list: companyProcedure
    .input(attendanceQuerySchema.optional().default({}))

list: companyProcedure
      .input(
        z
          .object({

recruiter: [
    "dashboard:view_admin",
    "people:view_company",
    "profile:view_self",
    "recruitment:view",
    "recruitment:manage",

findings.reproduction: Login as recruiter -> call attendance.list or leave.request.list -> returns company-wide records despite recruiter lacking attendance:view_company / leave:view_company.
findings.technicalRisk: Recruiter reads attendance (incl. last work location) and leave for the whole workforce.
findings.businessRisk: Within-tenant confidentiality violation of the stated capability matrix; the SEC-006 'systemic fix' is incomplete.
findings.recommendation: Swap companyProcedure -> requireCapability('attendance:view_company') on the four attendance reads and requireCapability('leave:view_company') on leave.request.list/balance.list (mirror the getSubtree fix already applied).
findings.workaround: None client-side.

findings.title: expense.markPaid callable by any staff role (finance action on companyProcedure)
findings.description: expense.markPaid transitions an approved expense to 'paid' (a finance close-out) but is guarded only by companyProcedure, so every non-employee/candidate staff role — including recruiter and department_manager, who hold no finance/payroll capability — can mark expenses paid. The in-code comment claims 'Only HR / super_admin can do this', which the guard does not enforce. SEC-004 fixed the read path but left this write over-permissive; the 03-permission-matrix.md flagged it as 'Over-permissive (any staff role)'.
findings.expectedBehavior: markPaid should be requireRole(super_admin, hr_manager, payroll_admin) / requireCapability('payroll:view_company') to match the finance-close intent.
findings.actualBehavior: recruiter and department_manager can move expenses to paid state.
findings.reproduction: Login as recruiter -> call expense.markPaid on an approved expense id -> status becomes 'paid'.
findings.technicalRisk: Unauthorized financial state transition by non-finance staff.
findings.businessRisk: Weak segregation of duties on expense payout; contradicts the documented finance-only intent.
findings.recommendation: Change expense.markPaid to requireRole(super_admin, hr_manager, payroll_admin) (the EXPENSE_COMPANY_ROLES already defined in this file) or requireCapability.
findings.workaround: None client-side.

findings.title: Alternate login route /api/auth/login bypasses account lockout and rate limiting (brute-force oracle)
findings.description: POST /api/auth/login performs a bcrypt password comparison against users.passwordHash and returns ok+user on success, but it bypasses every protection the NextAuth authorize() callback provides: the durable per-account lockout, the MFA gate, and the login rate-limit. The middleware only rate-limits /api/auth/callback/credentials (5/min) and general /api/auth (loose), and /api/auth/login is NOT in the middleware matcher at all, so no edge throttle applies. The route does not set a session cookie, so it is a password-verification oracle rather than a session bypass — but it enables unthrottled offline-style online password guessing that the account-lockout control (praised in the prior audit) was meant to stop.
findings.expectedBehavior: There should be a single authentication path (NextAuth credentials) that enforces lockout, MFA and rate-limiting; ad-hoc credential-check routes should not exist, or must apply the same lockout/rate-limit logic.
findings.actualBehavior: Unlimited unauthenticated password guesses against any account with no lockout, MFA or throttle.

evidence.snippet:
const isLoginAttempt = pathname.startsWith("/api/auth/callback/credentials");
  if (isLoginAttempt && !checkRateLimit(`login:${ip}`, 5, 60_000)) {

findings.reproduction: Script POST /api/auth/login with a fixed email and many passwords -> no lockout or 429 is ever returned; failed_login_attempts/locked_until are never consulted.
findings.technicalRisk: Online password brute force with the account-lockout control bypassed; credential-stuffing amplifier.
findings.businessRisk: Account takeover risk; the auth-hardening the 90/100 score credited is bypassable through this route.
findings.recommendation: Remove /api/auth/login if unused; otherwise route it through the same authorize() logic (lockout + MFA + rate-limit) and add it to the middleware matcher's login rate-limit branch.
findings.workaround: Add /api/auth/login to the login rate-limit matcher and block at edge.

findings.title: Focus outline removed with no replacement on Super Admin tenant filter input
findings.description: Across the codebase, every other instance of `focus:outline-none` is paired with a replacement indicator (`focus:ring-2`, `focus:border-*`, etc. — 28 of 29 occurrences checked). The tenant filter input on the Super Admin page is the sole exception: it removes the native outline and supplies no visible focus state at all.
findings.expectedBehavior: Keyboard users tabbing to the filter input should see a visible focus indicator (ring/border/box-shadow), consistent with every other input in the app.
findings.actualBehavior: Tabbing into the field shows no visible change; the input is indistinguishable from its unfocused state.

evidence.snippet: className="w-56 bg-transparent text-sm text-slate-800 placeholder:text-slate-400 focus:outline-none"

findings.reproduction: 1. Open /super-admin as a super_admin user. 2. Tab through the page until focus reaches the 'Filter by name, CR, schema' input. 3. No focus ring or border change is visible.
findings.technicalRisk: Trivial one-line fix.
findings.businessRisk: Keyboard-only super-admin operators (a small but high-privilege user group) can lose track of focus position on a page that controls tenant-level data.
findings.recommendation: Add `focus:ring-2 focus:ring-emerald-600` (or equivalent) matching the sibling filter/search inputs elsewhere in the app.
findings.workaround: Mouse users unaffected.

findings.title: Hand-rolled "Save Map View" modal lacks dialog semantics, focus trap and Escape-to-close
findings.wcagCriterion: 2.1.2 No Keyboard Trap (A) / 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A)
findings.description: The app already ships an accessible modal primitive (packages/ui/src/ui/dialog.tsx, built on @radix-ui/react-dialog, with built-in focus trap, Escape handling and aria-modal) and uses it correctly elsewhere (apps/web/app/(dashboard)/attendance/portal/page.tsx:249-328). The Guide Map page's "Save This View" flow instead renders a plain `fixed inset-0` overlay `<div>` with no `role="dialog"`, no `aria-modal="true"`, no focus trap, and no keydown handler for Escape.
findings.expectedBehavior: Opening 'Save This View' should trap focus inside the dialog, be announced as a dialog to screen readers, and close on Escape — matching the Punch Dialog pattern on /attendance/portal.
findings.actualBehavior: Tab can move focus out of the overlay into the page behind it; Escape does nothing; no dialog role is exposed.

evidence.snippet:
{showSaveDialog && (
  <div className="fixed inset-0 z-50 flex items-center justify-center bg-black/50">
    <Card className="w-96">

<Dialog open={!!selectedEmployee} onOpenChange={() => setSelectedEmployee(null)}>
  <DialogContent className="max-w-lg">

findings.reproduction: 1. Open /attendance/guide-map. 2. Click 'Save This View'. 3. Press Tab repeatedly — focus escapes into the sidebar/header behind the overlay. 4. Press Escape — nothing happens.
findings.technicalRisk: Low — swap the raw div for the existing `Dialog`/`DialogContent` components already imported elsewhere in the same file's sibling pages.
findings.businessRisk: Keyboard/screen-reader users saving a guide map location can get lost behind the overlay or be unable to dismiss it without a mouse.
findings.recommendation: Replace the plain overlay with `Dialog`/`DialogContent` from @hrms-app/ui, consistent with attendance/portal/page.tsx.
findings.workaround: Mouse/touch users can click Cancel or click outside conceptually (though click-outside isn't wired either, since it's not a real dialog).

findings.title: In-app help chat has no aria-live region for new messages
findings.description: The floating Taazur Help chat appends new assistant/user messages to a scrolling `<div>` with no `aria-live` attribute. A screen-reader user who submits a question and then reviews other parts of the page will not be notified when the AI's reply arrives.
findings.expectedBehavior: The message list (or a visually-hidden companion region) should be `aria-live="polite"` so new assistant replies are announced automatically, similar to how apps/web/app/(dashboard)/loading.tsx correctly uses `aria-live="polite"` for its skeleton state.
findings.actualBehavior: New messages (including the 'Thinking…' pending state) are silently inserted with no announcement.

evidence.snippet:
<div ref={scrollRef} className="flex-1 space-y-2.5 overflow-y-auto p-3 text-sm">
  {messages.map((m) => (

findings.reproduction: 1. Open the chat widget (bottom-right sparkle button). 2. Ask a question. 3. With a screen reader running, the reply appearing in the transcript is never announced.
findings.technicalRisk: Low — add `aria-live="polite" aria-relevant="additions"` to the scroll container, or a visually-hidden live region announcing the latest assistant message.
findings.businessRisk: The help chatbot is the app's self-service support channel; screen-reader users get a degraded, non-functional experience from the very feature meant to assist them.
findings.recommendation: Add `aria-live="polite"` to the message container (line 177).
findings.workaround: Users can manually re-focus/re-read the panel after sending a message.

findings.title: Destructive delete actions with zero confirmation step
findings.description: Two delete actions fire their mutation immediately on click with no confirmation of any kind — not even a native `confirm()`. This is inconsistent with the rest of the app, which guards comparable destructive actions (delete department, delete document, delete policy, revoke invite, cancel expense) behind a `window.confirm(...)` prompt.
findings.expectedBehavior: Deleting a saved guide map or a job requisition should require an explicit confirmation step before the mutation fires, matching the pattern used for departments/documents/policies elsewhere in the app.
findings.actualBehavior: A single click on the trash icon (guide map) or the 'Delete' button (job requisition) immediately deletes the record.

evidence.snippet:
<Button size="icon" variant="ghost" className="h-7 w-7 shrink-0 text-muted-foreground hover:text-red-500" onClick={() => deleteMutation.mutate(gm.id)}>
  <Trash2 className="h-3.5 w-3.5" />
</Button>

<Button variant="destructive" onClick={() => deleteMutation.mutate(job.id)}>
  Delete
</Button>
if (confirm("Delete this department?")) deleteMutation.mutate(node.id);

findings.reproduction: 1. Open /attendance/guide-map with at least one saved map. 2. Click the trash icon next to it. 3. The map is deleted immediately, no prompt. Similarly for a draft job requisition's Delete button on /recruitment/jobs/[id].
findings.technicalRisk: Low — add the same `confirm(...)` guard (or, better, a themed AlertDialog) used elsewhere in the codebase.
findings.businessRisk: A single misclick permanently deletes a saved guide map or a job requisition with no recovery path visible in the UI, risking accidental data loss for HR/recruiting workflows.
findings.recommendation: Add a confirmation step (ideally a themed AlertDialog rather than native confirm — see UX-010) before both mutations.
findings.workaround: None; deletion is immediate and irreversible from the UI.

findings.title: Password reset token written to server logs unconditionally; email delivery not wired
findings.description: request-reset builds the full reset URL containing the raw (un-hashed) token and logs it via console.info in ALL environments, including production - there is no NODE_ENV guard. Email delivery is a TODO, so in production the log is the ONLY place the token exists, and anyone with log/observability access (or a log sink integration) can complete an account takeover. Storing only the SHA-256 hash in the DB is good, but logging the plaintext token defeats it.
findings.expectedBehavior: The raw token must only ever leave the server inside the emailed link; it must never be written to logs. In non-prod, gate any debug logging behind an explicit flag.
findings.actualBehavior: console.info logs email + full reset URL (raw token) on every request-reset for an existing user, unconditionally.
findings.reproduction: POST /api/auth/request-reset with a registered email; inspect server logs -> the full reset URL with a usable raw token appears.
findings.technicalRisk: Account takeover for anyone able to read application logs.
findings.businessRisk: Privileged-account compromise; PDPL breach if logs are shipped to third-party sinks.
findings.recommendation: Remove the token log line; wire delivery through @hrms-app/email. If a dev fallback is needed, gate it behind NODE_ENV !== 'production' AND an explicit DEV flag, and log only that a link was generated (no token).
findings.workaround: Redact reset URLs at the log pipeline; treat existing logs as compromised and expire outstanding tokens.

findings.title: Payroll updateStatus allows state-machine shortcuts and an un-audited completed->cancelled that re-opens the period lock
findings.description: updateStatus accepts any target status from updatePayrollRunSchema. It blocks completed -> (non-completed/non-cancelled) but still permits completed -> cancelled with no reason and no audit, and permits forward jumps such as draft -> completed that skip pre_check/ready. Because the PAY-010 period lock exempts 'cancelled' runs, cancelling a completed run re-opens the month for a brand-new run, enabling a second payroll for the same period. Unlike the dedicated reopen mutation, updateStatus writes no audit record.
findings.expectedBehavior: Status transitions should follow the defined lifecycle (draft -> pre_check -> ready -> completed) with only the audited reopen able to unwind a completed run; any transition of a completed run should be audited.
findings.actualBehavior: Arbitrary forward jumps allowed; completed -> cancelled allowed silently with no audit; re-opens the period lock.

evidence.snippet: if (current.status === "completed" && input.data.status !== "completed" && input.data.status !== "cancelled") { throw FORBIDDEN }  // completed->cancelled slips through, no audit
if (existingRun && existingRun.status !== "cancelled") { throw CONFLICT ... }  // a cancelled run no longer blocks a new run for the period

findings.reproduction: On a completed run, call updateStatus { data:{ status:'cancelled' } } (succeeds, no audit). Then payroll.run.create for the same periodMonth succeeds because the prior run is 'cancelled'.
findings.technicalRisk: Undefined/loose state machine; audit gap on a completed-run transition.
findings.businessRisk: A completed month can be silently re-run, risking double payment, with no audit trail of the cancellation.
findings.recommendation: Enforce explicit allowed transitions; route any completed-run change exclusively through the audited reopen (with reason); write an audit record for every status change; do not treat 'cancelled' as unlocking a period that already produced payslips.
findings.workaround: Operationally forbid using updateStatus on completed runs; use reopen only.

findings.title: payslip.create allows manual injection of arbitrary payslip amounts, bypassing the calculation engine and audit
findings.description: payslip.create accepts a client array of payslips (basic, housing, netPay, gosi, etc.) and inserts them directly with no recomputation against the GOSI/orchestrator engine, no verification that the run exists or is open, and no audit entry. Amounts are only bounded by the schema (basic/netPay positive, others >= 0), so arbitrarily large net-pay figures can be written straight into a run.
findings.expectedBehavior: Payslip amounts should be produced by the orchestrator (as run.create does) or, for manual corrections, validated against the engine and audited, tied to a run in an editable state.
findings.actualBehavior: Raw client values are inserted verbatim; no engine cross-check, no audit.

evidence.snippet: netPay: z.number().positive().describe("Net pay after all calculations"),  // no upper bound / engine check

findings.reproduction: Call payroll.payslip.create with a payslip whose netPay = 999999 for any employeeId/payrollRunId. It is inserted unchanged.
findings.technicalRisk: Payslip figures can diverge from the statutory engine; no consistency guard.
findings.businessRisk: Inflated or fabricated net pay and GOSI figures enter the payroll ledger with no audit trail.
findings.recommendation: Remove or lock down the raw create path; require corrections to go through an audited, engine-validated adjustment tied to a non-completed run.
findings.workaround: Disable payslip.create in the UI; only create payslips via run.create.

findings.title: Compensation adjustment can be self-approved in a single call (client-supplied status + approvedById)
findings.description: createCompensationAdjustmentSchema lets the caller set both status and approvedById directly, and the create mutation inserts them verbatim. An hr_manager can create a salary adjustment already flagged status='approved' with approvedById = self in one request, with no separate approval workflow, no actor != approver check, and no audit entry.
findings.expectedBehavior: A compensation adjustment should be created as 'proposed', approved by a distinct authorised approver, and the approval should be audited; approvedById should be set by the server from the approving session, not the client.
findings.actualBehavior: status and approvedById are accepted from the client and stored as-is; no approval step, no audit.

evidence.snippet: export const createCompensationAdjustmentSchema = z.object({ planId, employeeId, adjustmentType, currentValue, proposedValue, status: z.string().optional(), approvedById: z.string().uuid().optional(), ... });

create: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager").input(createCompensationAdjustmentSchema).mutation(async ({ ctx, input }) => { const [item] = await ctx.db.insert(schema.tenant.compensationAdjustments).values(input).returning(); return item; })

findings.reproduction: As hr_manager, call retention.compensationAdjustment.create with status='approved', approvedById = own employee/user id, proposedValue = large amount. It is stored as an approved adjustment.
findings.technicalRisk: Approval fields are client-controlled; no SoD.
findings.businessRisk: Unilateral, un-audited salary increases; a manager can self-approve compensation changes.
findings.recommendation: Force status='proposed' on create, ignore client approvedById, add a separate approve mutation requiring a different authorised approver, and audit the approval.
findings.workaround: Restrict comp-adjustment approval to super_admin and review manually.

findings.title: CSP allows script-src 'unsafe-inline' (and style-src 'unsafe-inline'), defeating XSS containment
findings.url: N/A (applies to every response via headers())
findings.description: The Content-Security-Policy sets script-src and style-src to include 'unsafe-inline'. 'unsafe-inline' on script-src means any injected inline <script> or event-handler attribute executes, so the CSP provides no meaningful defence-in-depth against XSS. For a PII app (iqama, IBAN, salary) an XSS payload could read the DOM/tRPC responses and exfiltrate. 'unsafe-eval' was correctly dropped and the code comment acknowledges a pending nonce rollout, but until nonces ship the primary XSS mitigation is absent.
findings.expectedBehavior: script-src should use per-request nonces or hashes (no 'unsafe-inline'); style-src should move to nonces/hashes where feasible.
findings.actualBehavior: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' and style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' are hard-coded.

evidence.snippet: "script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'",
"style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'",

findings.reproduction: Load any page, inspect the Content-Security-Policy response header; observe script-src includes 'unsafe-inline'.
findings.technicalRisk: A single stored/reflected XSS becomes fully weaponizable (session/PII exfiltration) with no CSP brake.
findings.businessRisk: PDPL breach exposure if employee PII is exfiltrated via injected script; reputational damage.
findings.recommendation: Adopt Next.js nonce-based CSP (middleware sets a per-request nonce, propagate to inline scripts) and remove 'unsafe-inline' from script-src. Migrate inline styles to nonces/hashes or external stylesheets.
findings.workaround: Keep object-src 'none', frame-ancestors 'none', base-uri 'self' (already present) and prioritise the nonce rollout the comment references.

findings.title: In-memory rate limiter is per-instance and unbounded; CSRF skipped when Origin absent
findings.description: middleware.ts implements rate limiting with a module-level Map (rateLimitMap). On serverless/edge (Vercel) each instance has its own Map, so limits are per-instance and largely ineffective under horizontal scaling; the Map also never evicts expired entries (unbounded memory growth). Additionally, the same-origin CSRF check only rejects when an Origin header is present and mismatched - requests with no Origin header (e.g. non-browser clients) bypass the check.
findings.expectedBehavior: Rate limiting should use a shared store (Redis) so limits hold across instances, evict expired keys, and CSRF/same-origin protection should fail-closed for state-changing requests lacking a trustworthy Origin.
findings.actualBehavior: Limits reset per instance and per deploy; Map grows unbounded; missing Origin header skips the CSRF check.

evidence.snippet: const rateLimitMap = new Map<string, { count: number; resetAt: number }>();  // per-instance, no eviction
if (origin) { try { if (new URL(origin).host !== host) return 403; } catch { return 403; } }  // no origin -> check skipped

findings.reproduction: Send state-changing POSTs to /api/upload without an Origin header (curl) -> CSRF check is bypassed; distribute login attempts so they land on different lambda instances -> per-IP limit is not enforced globally.
findings.technicalRisk: Rate limiting ineffective at scale; memory leak; CSRF bypass for non-browser clients.
findings.businessRisk: Weakened brute-force/DoS/CSRF protections on sensitive endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Back rate limiting with Redis (already a dependency via BullMQ) keyed by IP+route; evict expired keys; require a valid Origin/Referer (fail-closed) for state-changing custom routes, or rely on NextAuth CSRF tokens.
findings.workaround: Add an edge/WAF rate limit; require Origin header on state-changing routes.

findings.title: No "skip to main content" link before the sidebar navigation
findings.description: There is no skip link anywhere in the codebase (confirmed via repo-wide search). Every dashboard page places roughly 15-20 sidebar navigation links (Sidebar's `<nav>`, apps/web/components/sidebar.tsx:158-184) before the `<main>` landmark in the DOM order, with no mechanism for keyboard/screen-reader users to jump directly to page content.
findings.expectedBehavior: A visually-hidden-until-focused 'Skip to main content' link should be the first focusable element, targeting the `<main>` element in dashboard-shell.tsx:60.
findings.actualBehavior: Keyboard users must tab through the entire sidebar nav (and header controls) on every single page load/navigation before reaching page content.

evidence.snippet:
<Header user={user} onOpenMenu={() => setMobileOpen(true)} />
<main className="mx-auto w-full max-w-[1680px] p-4 sm:p-6 lg:p-8">{children}</main>
<nav className="flex-1 overflow-y-auto px-3 pb-4 ...">

findings.reproduction: Load any dashboard page and press Tab from the top — the first ~20 stops are sidebar links, not page content.
findings.technicalRisk: Trivial to add.
findings.businessRisk: Adds friction for keyboard-only and screen-reader users on every navigation, especially painful for people who use the app throughout the workday (e.g. HR staff processing many records).
findings.recommendation: Add a standard skip link as the first child of DashboardShell's root, pointing to an `id` on the `<main>` element.
findings.workaround: None; mouse/touch users unaffected.

findings.title: leave_balances has no uniqueness on (employee_id, leave_type_id, year) and no timestamps — duplicate balances / leave double-counting possible
findings.exploitability: A retry/race in balance creation, or a bug, can insert two rows for the same employee/type/year; consumers may sum or pick arbitrarily.
findings.description: leave_balances defines id, employeeId (FK), leaveTypeId (FK), balance numeric(5,1), year integer — with no unique constraint across (employee_id, leave_type_id, year) and no created_at/updated_at. Nothing at the DB level prevents duplicate balance rows for the same key, and there is no audit of when a balance changed.
findings.expectedBehavior: Exactly one balance row per employee per leave type per year, enforced by a unique constraint, with change timestamps.
findings.actualBehavior: Duplicates are permitted; no temporal audit columns.

evidence.snippet: export const leaveBalances = pgTable('leave_balances', { id, employeeId, leaveTypeId, balance: numeric('balance',{precision:5,scale:1}).notNull(), year: integer('year').notNull() });  // no unique(), no timestamps
CREATE TABLE "leave_balances" ( "id" ..., "employee_id" uuid NOT NULL, "leave_type_id" uuid NOT NULL, "balance" numeric(5,1) NOT NULL, "year" integer NOT NULL );

findings.reproduction: Insert two rows with identical employee_id, leave_type_id, year -> both succeed.
findings.technicalRisk: Ambiguous/duplicated balances; consumers may double-count or read the wrong row.
findings.businessRisk: Incorrect leave entitlements/accruals; disputes.
findings.recommendation: Add UNIQUE (employee_id, leave_type_id, year) and created_at/updated_at columns; dedupe existing data before applying. Include in provisioning DDL (DB-001).
findings.workaround: Enforce single-row invariant in application upsert logic (ON CONFLICT once the unique index exists).

findings.title: PII '_enc' columns are plaintext TEXT at the DB level — encryption is enforced only by the app-layer Drizzle codec
findings.exploitability: Any write path that bypasses the Drizzle encryptedText codec (raw SQL, seed scripts, psql, db.execute, bulk import) stores plaintext PII in a column whose name implies it is encrypted.
findings.description: iqama_number_enc / passport_number_enc / bank_iban_enc are ordinary TEXT columns; confidentiality depends entirely on every writer going through the encryptedText customType. The codec even passes through non-marked (legacy plaintext) values on read, so a plaintext write is invisible on read and silently defeats encryption-at-rest. Seed scripts and any db.execute raw path are realistic bypasses.
findings.expectedBehavior: PII-at-rest guarantee should not depend on developer discipline; ideally a DB-level guard (e.g., a CHECK/trigger requiring the encv1: marker) or centralized write path.
findings.actualBehavior: Plaintext can land in _enc columns with no error and no read-time signal.

evidence.snippet:
iqama_number_enc TEXT,
      passport_number_enc TEXT,
      bank_iban_enc TEXT,
export function decryptField(stored: string): string { if (!stored.startsWith(MARKER)) return stored; // legacy plaintext passes through

findings.reproduction: INSERT into employees (iqama_number_enc) VALUES ('1234567890') via raw SQL; row reads back as plaintext with isEncrypted=false and no error.
findings.technicalRisk: Silent plaintext PII at rest via any non-codec writer.
findings.businessRisk: PDPL/PII exposure that passes unnoticed; audits may wrongly assume all _enc data is encrypted.
findings.recommendation: Add a DB CHECK/trigger enforcing the encv1: marker on _enc columns (allowing a time-boxed legacy window), or route all writes through a single repository. Add a lint/test that fails if raw writes target _enc columns. Complete the encrypt-pii-backfill and then tighten the constraint.
findings.workaround: Code review to ensure only Drizzle codec writes these columns; run backfill.

findings.title: Destructive-action confirmations rely on unstyled native window.confirm() instead of the app's own dialog
findings.description: Six separate destructive flows use the browser-native, unstyled `confirm()` dialog rather than the app's own themed Dialog component (already available in packages/ui and used for the Punch Dialog on /attendance/portal). Native `confirm()` cannot be localized to match the user's chosen Arabic/English preference, doesn't match the Saudi-luxury visual language, and blocks the JS main thread.
findings.expectedBehavior: Destructive confirmations should use the shared Dialog/AlertDialog pattern, with buttons/copy that respect `preferredLanguage`.
findings.actualBehavior: Confirmations show the browser's default OK/Cancel dialog with English-only, unstyled text regardless of the app's current language setting.

evidence.snippet: if (confirm("Revoke this invitation?")) revokeInvite.mutate({ inviteId: id });

if (confirm(`Delete "${p.name}"?`)) {
  deleteMutation.mutate(p.id);
onClick={() => { if (confirm("Cancel this expense?")) cancel.mutate(row.id); }}

findings.reproduction: Switch the app to Arabic, then trigger any of the listed delete/cancel/revoke actions — the confirmation dialog remains in English with no theming.
findings.technicalRisk: Low functional risk; purely a consistency/i18n gap.
findings.businessRisk: Minor brand/consistency erosion; not a blocker to task completion.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a shared `useConfirm()`/`ConfirmDialog` built on the existing Dialog primitive and migrate these six call sites to it.
findings.workaround: None needed — functionally works today.

findings.title: Internal error strings leaked to clients (qiwa, health)
findings.description: Several REST handlers return raw error text to the caller. /api/qiwa returns error.message (which can include upstream Qiwa/DB detail) and /api/health returns String(err) on failure. In contrast the tRPC error formatter and the login/signup/reset routes correctly return generic messages. This leaks internal detail (hostnames, driver errors) to unauthenticated clients.
findings.expectedBehavior: Client-facing errors should be generic; detailed errors logged server-side only (as done in /api/auth/login).
findings.actualBehavior: Raw error.message / String(err) is serialized into the HTTP response body.

evidence.snippet: } catch (error) { return NextResponse.json({ error: error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error) }, { status: 500 }); }
} catch (err) { return NextResponse.json({ error: String(err) }, { status: 500 }); }

findings.reproduction: Trigger a DB/upstream error against /api/health or /api/qiwa and inspect the response body for internal error text.
findings.technicalRisk: Leaks internal implementation/infrastructure detail useful for reconnaissance.
findings.businessRisk: Assists attacker reconnaissance; minor confidentiality impact.
findings.recommendation: Replace error bodies with a generic message and log the detail via console.error server-side.
findings.workaround: Wrap responses with a generic error string.

findings.title: Retention update schemas allow client to set employeeId/status via input.data spread
findings.description: The retention router applies updates with `.set({ ...input.data, updatedAt })` across ~40 procedures. Several update schemas in packages/validators/src/retention.ts include mutable identity/state fields such as employeeId (optional) and status (optional). Because the whole validated object is spread into .set(), a caller can reassign a record to a different employeeId or force a status transition directly, bypassing the intended workflow. Department write-scoping helpers exist (scoping.ts assertManagesGoal/Review/...) and procedures are capability-gated, which limits blast radius, but the schemas should not expose employeeId/status as freely-settable update fields. Marked suspected pending confirmation that each update procedure calls the corresponding assert* helper.
findings.expectedBehavior: Update DTOs should allowlist only the fields a caller may change; immutable ownership keys (employeeId) and workflow state (status) should be excluded from generic update schemas or handled via dedicated, guarded transitions.
findings.actualBehavior: employeeId and status are present in update schemas and spread straight into .set().

evidence.snippet: .set({ ...input.data, updatedAt: new Date() }).where(eq(...id, input.id))  // spreads employeeId/status from client

findings.reproduction: Call a retention update mutation with data.employeeId set to another employee's UUID (or data.status set to a terminal state) and observe the record reassigned/transitioned.
findings.technicalRisk: Record reassignment across employees; unguarded status transitions.
findings.businessRisk: Performance/retention data integrity (goals, reviews, PIPs) can be silently reassigned or force-completed.
findings.recommendation: Remove employeeId (and status where it should be a guarded transition) from the *Update schemas, or explicitly pick allowed fields before .set(). Ensure every update calls the matching assertManages* scoping helper.
findings.workaround: Add explicit field picking in each retention update before writing.

findings.title: MFA lacks recovery/backup codes and per-account TOTP attempt throttling
findings.description: MFA enrollment stores only a TOTP secret; there are no backup/recovery codes, so a user who loses their authenticator is locked out and requires manual DB intervention (clearing mfa_secret). Additionally, a wrong TOTP at login deliberately does not count toward account lockout (the password was correct), and the only throttle on TOTP guessing is the per-instance IP rate limit (see AUTH-007). With a +/-1 step window (3 valid codes per 1,000,000) online guessing is impractical but not rate-limited at the account level.
findings.expectedBehavior: Provide single-use hashed backup codes at enrollment and throttle repeated failed TOTP attempts per account.
findings.actualBehavior: No backup codes; TOTP failures are unbounded at the account level.

evidence.snippet:
// MFA gate (C4) ... A wrong/missing code
        // does not count toward lockout (the password was correct).

findings.reproduction: Enrol MFA; there is no recovery-code output. Submit repeated wrong TOTP codes with a correct password - no account-level lockout accrues.
findings.technicalRisk: TOTP guessing has only a weak IP throttle; account recovery requires manual DB edits.
findings.businessRisk: Support burden and lockouts; slightly weakened MFA.
findings.recommendation: Generate 8-10 single-use backup codes (store hashed) at enrollment; add a per-account failed-TOTP counter that triggers a temporary MFA lock.
findings.workaround: Document an admin runbook to reset mfa_secret on request after identity verification.

findings.title: Self-serve signup mints a tenant super_admin with no email verification or CR validation
findings.description: Signup creates a tenant and inserts a user with role 'super_admin' and no email verification (emailVerified is never set) and no CR-number validation. Anyone can register a tenant bound to an email they do not control. Blast radius is limited because super_admin is tenant-scoped and cross-tenant registry access is separately gated by the PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist (good), so the new super_admin only controls its own freshly created (empty) tenant - but it does enable spam-tenant creation and unverified-email accounts. There is also no meaningful anti-automation on /api/auth/signup (only the loose 10/sec auth-route limit).
findings.expectedBehavior: Require email verification before activating the account, validate the CR number format, and rate-limit signups.
findings.actualBehavior: super_admin created immediately, unverified email, no CR validation.
findings.reproduction: POST /api/auth/signup with any email -> a tenant + super_admin account is created with emailVerified null.
findings.technicalRisk: Unverified privileged accounts; tenant/DB-schema spam (each signup creates a new schema).
findings.businessRisk: Resource abuse and unverified-identity accounts in a compliance-sensitive product.
findings.recommendation: Add email verification before first login, validate CR number, and rate-limit signup (shared store).
findings.workaround: Monitor tenant creation; disable open signup if not required.

findings.title: Leave requests can be back-dated: no lower bound on start date
findings.description: createLeaveRequestSchema only enforces endDate >= startDate; there is no minimum date. A request can be dated entirely in the past, decrementing the prior period's balance (the decrement year is derived from startDate), which can conflict with already-closed attendance/payroll periods.
findings.expectedBehavior: Back-dated leave beyond a configurable grace window should be rejected or require explicit HR override, and should not silently affect a closed period.
findings.actualBehavior: Any past startDate is accepted; year is taken from startDate for the balance effect.

evidence.snippet: .refine((data) => data.endDate >= data.startDate, { message: "End date must be on or after start date", path: ["endDate"] });  // no min date
const year = new Date(current.startDate).getUTCFullYear();  // balance effect keyed to (possibly past) startDate

findings.reproduction: Create a leave request with startDate/endDate in a prior month; it is accepted and, on approval, debits that year's balance.
findings.technicalRisk: Past-period data mutation.
findings.businessRisk: Retroactive leave can mask absences or manipulate a closed period's records.
findings.recommendation: Add a minimum-date rule (e.g. startDate >= today - N days) and block edits to closed periods; require HR override with audit for exceptions.
findings.workaround: HR review of any request with a past start date.

findings.title: Manager can author their own performance review/response (no actor != subject check)
findings.description: review.create and reviewResponse.create for a department_manager are gated only by assertManagesEmployee / assertManagesReview (department scope). A manager whose own employee record is in a department they head can create or respond to a performance review about themselves, since the scope check passes. There is no check that the reviewer differs from the review subject.
findings.expectedBehavior: A manager should not author or score their own performance review; self as subject should be rejected and escalated.
findings.actualBehavior: Only department-scope is enforced; actor != subject is not.

evidence.snippet: create: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager", "department_manager").input(createReviewSchema).mutation(async ({ ctx, input }) => { await assertManagesEmployee(ctx, input.employeeId); const [item] = await ctx.db.insert(schema.tenant.reviews).values(input).returning(); ... })

export async function assertManagesEmployee(ctx, employeeId) { if (ctx.user.role !== "department_manager") return; ... // passes when the subject is in a dept the caller heads, including the caller themselves }

findings.reproduction: As a department_manager who is a member of a department they head, call review.create with employeeId = own id. It succeeds.
findings.technicalRisk: Review authorship is scope-based, not identity-aware.
findings.businessRisk: Managers can write favourable self-reviews, corrupting talent/comp decisions that rely on review data.
findings.recommendation: Reject when input.employeeId (or the review's employeeId) equals the caller's employeeId; require a skip-level reviewer for a manager's own review.
findings.workaround: HR review of any self-authored review rows.

findings.title: Approval/payout/leave transitions lack an atomic status guard (race-condition double-processing)
findings.description: State-changing updates read the current status, then update WHERE id = ... (not WHERE id AND status = expected). Two concurrent requests can both pass the status check and both apply the transition (e.g. approve+approve, or markPaid twice, or approve leave twice decrementing the balance twice). The guards are read-then-write rather than a conditional atomic update.
findings.expectedBehavior: Transitions should use an atomic conditional update (WHERE id AND status = expected) or a transaction/row lock so only one concurrent actor can perform the transition.
findings.actualBehavior: Read-check-then-unconditional-update by id; no atomic guard.

evidence.snippet: const [updated] = await ctx.db.update(schema.tenant.expenses).set({ status: "paid", paidAt: new Date() }).where(eq(schema.tenant.expenses.id, input.id)).returning();  // WHERE id only
const [request] = await ctx.db.update(schema.tenant.leaveRequests).set(input.data).where(eq(schema.tenant.leaveRequests.id, input.id)).returning();  // then balance is adjusted; no atomic guard

findings.reproduction: Fire two concurrent expense.markPaid calls for the same approved expense; both can read 'approved' before either writes 'paid', so both proceed (and any downstream payment fires twice).
findings.technicalRisk: Non-atomic transitions permit double-processing under concurrency.
findings.businessRisk: Potential double payment / double balance decrement under race conditions.
findings.recommendation: Use conditional updates (.where(and(eq(id), eq(status, expected)))) and treat a zero-row result as 'already processed'; wrap balance-affecting leave approval in a transaction.
findings.workaround: Low real-world likelihood at current scale; prioritise after the higher-severity SoD/calc items.

findings.title: Permissive CSP connect-src 'https:' and img-src 'https:' allow PII exfiltration to any HTTPS host
findings.description: connect-src permits 'https:' (any TLS origin) and img-src permits 'https:' (any TLS origin). Combined with script-src 'unsafe-inline' (PRIV-001), an injected script can beacon PII to an attacker-controlled https endpoint (fetch or an pixel with data in the query string) without CSP interference.
findings.expectedBehavior: connect-src and img-src should enumerate the specific origins the app actually needs (self + Supabase + Perplexity CDN + Sentry).
findings.actualBehavior: connect-src 'self' https: and img-src 'self' blob: data: https: are wide open to all HTTPS hosts.

evidence.snippet: "img-src 'self' blob: data: https:",
"connect-src 'self' https:",

findings.reproduction: Inspect CSP header; note connect-src/img-src accept arbitrary https origins.
findings.technicalRisk: Removes CSP's exfiltration-channel restriction; any injected code can phone home.
findings.businessRisk: Amplifies impact of any XSS into a data-exfiltration event.
findings.recommendation: Replace 'https:' with an explicit allow-list of required origins for connect-src and img-src.
findings.workaround: N/A

findings.title: User email placed in reset URL query string (leaks via history / referrer)
findings.description: The reset link carries the user's email as a query parameter. Query strings land in browser history, proxy logs, and (for any off-site navigation) the Referer header. Email is PII under PDPL and need not be in the URL — the token alone identifies the request server-side.
findings.expectedBehavior: Identify the reset by token only; resolve the email server-side from the token record.
findings.actualBehavior: email is included as a query parameter alongside the token.

evidence.snippet: const resetUrl = `${base}/reset-password?email=${encodeURIComponent(email)}&token=${rawToken}`;

findings.reproduction: Generate a reset link; observe email present in the URL.
findings.technicalRisk: Email disclosure via history/referrer.
findings.businessRisk: Minor PDPL data-minimisation gap.
findings.recommendation: Drop the email param; look it up from the verificationTokens row by token hash during reset.
findings.workaround: N/A

findings.title: Authentication failures log user email (PII) via console, bypassing the redacting logger
findings.description: Failed-login and MFA-failure paths log the account email through console.warn. A redacting pino logger exists in packages/config (redacts authorization/cookie/password) but is not used here, and its redact list does not cover email or PII fields anyway. Emails are PII and accumulate in logs on every failed attempt.
findings.expectedBehavior: Log a hashed/opaque account identifier, not the raw email; route through the redacting logger.
findings.actualBehavior: console.warn('[auth] failed login attempt for', email) and MFA-failure log the email.

evidence.snippet: console.warn('[auth] failed login attempt for', email);
console.warn('[auth] MFA verification failed for', email);
redact: ["req.headers.authorization", "req.headers.cookie", "body.password"],

findings.reproduction: Trigger a failed login; observe email in server logs.
findings.technicalRisk: PII (and enumeration signal) accumulates in logs.
findings.businessRisk: PDPL data-minimisation / log-hygiene gap.
findings.recommendation: Remove email from these log lines (or hash it) and route auth logging through the redacting logger; extend redact paths to cover PII.
findings.workaround: N/A

findings.title: Icon-only "clear search" button has no accessible name
findings.description: The small 'X' button that clears the tenant filter text renders only a lucide `<X>` icon with no `aria-label`, so it has no accessible name.
findings.expectedBehavior: The button should expose an accessible name, e.g. `aria-label="Clear filter"`.
findings.actualBehavior: Screen readers announce it as an unlabeled 'button'.

evidence.snippet:
<button onClick={() => setSearch("")} className="rounded-full p-0.5 text-slate-400 hover:text-slate-700">
  <X className="h-3 w-3" />
</button>

findings.reproduction: Type text into the tenant filter, tab to the clear (X) button, listen with a screen reader — it announces only 'button'.
findings.technicalRisk: Trivial.
findings.businessRisk: Minor friction for screen-reader-using admins.
findings.recommendation: Add `aria-label="Clear filter"` to the button.
findings.workaround: Sighted users can infer purpose from the icon.

findings.title: Shared Table component's header cells omit explicit scope="col"
findings.page: All tables (Employees, Documents, Compliance, Qiwa, Payroll, Retention, etc.)
findings.description: The shared `TableHead` renders a bare `<th>` with no `scope` attribute. Most browsers/screen readers infer `scope="col"` for `<th>` inside a `<thead>`, so this is not a hard failure, but explicit scope is recommended best practice, particularly for the app's data-dense HR tables which are otherwise the most tabular-heavy content in the product.
findings.expectedBehavior: `TableHead` should default to `scope="col"` (with an override prop for any future row-header use).
findings.actualBehavior: No scope attribute is emitted.
findings.reproduction: Inspect any TableHead render (e.g. /employees) — the `<th>` has no `scope` attribute.
findings.technicalRisk: Trivial, single shared-component change benefits every table in the app.
findings.businessRisk: Minor; most screen readers already infer column headers correctly.
findings.recommendation: Default `scope="col"` in TableHead, consumed once by all ~15+ tables in the app.
findings.workaround: None needed; most AT infers this correctly already.

findings.title: AI chat forwards client-supplied model string to the LLM provider
findings.description: ai.chat.send passes input.model (validated only as z.string().min(1).optional()) straight to getLlmClient().complete({ model: input.model }). If packages/llm does not allowlist the model, a client could request an arbitrary or more expensive model, driving up cost. The message array is well-bounded (max 20 messages, 8000 chars each) and temperature is capped, which limits other abuse.
findings.expectedBehavior: The model override should be constrained to a server-side allowlist of approved models.
findings.actualBehavior: Any non-empty model string is forwarded to the provider.

evidence.snippet: model: z.string().min(1).optional(),  // no enum / allowlist
const res = await client.complete({ system: systemPrompt, messages: ..., model: input.model });

findings.reproduction: Call ai.chat.send with model set to an expensive/unexpected model id and observe whether packages/llm rejects it.
findings.technicalRisk: Cost inflation / provider abuse if model is unrestricted.
findings.businessRisk: LLM spend abuse.
findings.recommendation: Validate model against z.enum([...approvedModels]) or resolve it server-side per topic; ignore client model unless it is in the allowlist.
findings.workaround: Drop input.model server-side and pick the model per provider.

findings.title: company/profile PATCH and company/invite POST parse body without Zod
findings.description: Both handlers destructure request.json() manually instead of validating with a Zod schema. company/profile PATCH writes industry/companySize/website with no length/format bounds (unbounded strings). company/invite POST validates email only via `email?.trim()` (no email-format check) though it does allowlist the role. Both are auth+role gated so the risk is limited to authenticated tenant admins, but this deviates from the project's Zod-everywhere convention and permits unbounded/malformed data.
findings.expectedBehavior: All request bodies validated with a Zod schema (bounded strings, emailSchema).
findings.actualBehavior: Manual destructuring; no schema; unbounded strings; email format unchecked on invite.

evidence.snippet:
const body = await request.json();
  const { industry, companySize, website } = body;  // no Zod, unbounded

const { email, role = "employee" } = body;
  if (!email?.trim()) return 400;  // no email-format validation

findings.reproduction: PATCH /api/company/profile with a multi-megabyte website string, or POST /api/company/invite with email='not-an-email' -> accepted.
findings.technicalRisk: Unbounded string storage; malformed invite emails.
findings.businessRisk: Minor data-quality/storage issues; low.
findings.recommendation: Introduce Zod schemas (bounded strings + emailSchema) and safeParse the body, matching signup/reset routes.
findings.workaround: Add length caps and an email regex check inline.

findings.title: Password-reset link (raw token) logged to server console in all environments
findings.description: request-reset generates a single-use reset token and, because email delivery is still a TODO, logs the full reset URL (including the raw token) via console.info for every request in every environment. Anyone with access to server/log aggregation can read valid reset tokens and take over accounts within the token TTL (1 hour).
findings.expectedBehavior: Reset tokens/links must never be logged; deliver via the email package. Any diagnostic logging should be gated to non-production and must not include the raw token.
findings.actualBehavior: console.info logs email + full reset URL with the raw token unconditionally.

evidence.snippet: console.info("[auth] password reset link generated for", email, resetUrl);  // resetUrl contains rawToken

findings.reproduction: Trigger a reset for a known email and read the reset URL (with token) from server logs; use it to reset the password.
findings.technicalRisk: Valid password-reset tokens exposed in logs.
findings.businessRisk: Account takeover for anyone with log access; PDPL/audit concern.
findings.recommendation: Remove the token from logs; wire up @hrms-app/email delivery. If a dev fallback is needed, gate it to NODE_ENV!=production and log only that a link was generated (no token).
findings.workaround: Delete the console.info line and rely on email delivery.

findings.title: Reset token transmitted in URL query string (history / Referer / access-log leakage)
findings.category: Password Recovery / Sensitive Data in URL
findings.description: The reset link carries the raw token (and email) as URL query parameters. Tokens in URLs are recorded in browser history, may leak via the Referer header to any third-party asset on the reset page, and land in server/proxy access logs. Token TTL (1h), single-use and hashed storage limit the window, but query-string delivery is discouraged.
findings.expectedBehavior: Prefer delivering the token via a path segment with a strict no-referrer policy, or accept the token in a POST body / fragment; ensure Referrer-Policy: no-referrer on the reset page.
findings.actualBehavior: email + raw token are in the query string and read client-side from useSearchParams.

evidence.snippet: const resetUrl = `${base}/reset-password?email=${encodeURIComponent(email)}&token=${rawToken}`;

const email = params.get("email") ?? "";
  const token = params.get("token") ?? "";

findings.reproduction: Open a reset link; the token is visible in the address bar and browser history and is sent as Referer to any external resource loaded by the page.
findings.technicalRisk: Token leakage via history/Referer/logs within the 1h window.
findings.businessRisk: Marginal ATO risk if a reset URL leaks.
findings.recommendation: Set Referrer-Policy: no-referrer on the reset route and keep the reset page free of third-party assets; consider a path-based token.
findings.workaround: Short TTL (already 1h) and single-use (already enforced) limit exposure.

findings.title: Diagnostics endpoint discloses env wiring (AUTH_SECRET length, DB host/port) outside production
findings.description: The debug endpoint returns AUTH_SECRET length, AUTH_URL/NEXTAUTH_URL values, DB hostname and port, NODE_ENV and demo-mode state to anonymous callers. It is correctly disabled in production unless ENABLE_DEBUG_ENDPOINT=true (good), but in any non-production/preview environment it is fully open and leaks internal hostnames and the secret length. Preview deployments of a SaaS are frequently internet-reachable.
findings.expectedBehavior: Diagnostics should require authentication (platform-operator) even in non-prod, and should never echo secret metadata such as AUTH_SECRET length.
findings.actualBehavior: Anonymous GET returns env wiring in all non-prod environments.

evidence.snippet:
hasAuthSecret: !!authSecret,
    authSecretLength: authSecret?.length ?? 0,
    ...
    hasDbUrl: !!dbUrl,
    dbHost,
    dbPort,

findings.reproduction: GET /api/auth/debug on a preview/staging deployment -> returns dbHost, dbPort, authSecretLength, etc.
findings.technicalRisk: Recon aid (internal hostnames, secret length) on non-prod environments.
findings.businessRisk: Low; supports targeting of infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: Require platform-operator auth for this endpoint in all environments and drop authSecretLength/dbHost/dbPort from the payload.
findings.workaround: Ensure preview deployments are access-controlled.

findings.title: Offer outcome transitions (send/accept/decline) set status unconditionally with no state-machine guard
findings.description: The offer send/accept/decline mutations each set the status directly by id with no check of the current status. An offer can be moved to 'accepted' without ever being 'sent', re-accepted after being declined, or flipped between accepted and declined, and update can set any status with side-effect timestamps. There is no enforced draft -> sent -> accepted/declined lifecycle.
findings.expectedBehavior: Only a 'sent' offer should be acceptable/declinable; terminal states (accepted/declined) should be immutable; illegal transitions rejected.
findings.actualBehavior: Status is overwritten unconditionally regardless of prior state.
