evidence.snippet: accept: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager").input(z.object({ id: z.string().uuid() })).mutation(async ({ ctx, input }) => { const [offer] = await ctx.db.update(schema.tenant.offers).set({ status: "accepted", acceptedAt: new Date(), ... }).where(eq(...)).returning(); ... })  // no current-state check
decline: requireRole("super_admin", "hr_manager") ... .set({ status: "declined", declinedAt: new Date(), declineReason: input.reason, ... })  // can run even on an accepted offer

findings.reproduction: Create an offer (status draft), call offer.accept -> accepted without being sent; then call offer.decline -> declined. Both succeed.
findings.technicalRisk: Offer records can hold inconsistent state/timestamps.
findings.businessRisk: Mis-recorded hiring outcomes; a declined offer can be flipped to accepted (or vice versa) with no guard, affecting onboarding/headcount reporting.
findings.recommendation: Guard each transition on the required prior status and make accepted/declined terminal; reject illegal transitions with a clear error.
findings.workaround: Operational discipline; review offer audit history.

findings.title: Referral creation trusts client-supplied referrerEmployeeId and rewardAmount (self-crediting)
findings.description: referral.create is a bare protectedProcedure and inserts input directly; createReferralSchema carries a required client-supplied referrerEmployeeId and an optional rewardAmount. A user can create a referral crediting any employee (including themselves) and propose the reward amount. The reward payout transition (status='reward_paid') is HR-gated via referral.update, so money is not moved directly, but the referrer-of-record and proposed amount are attacker-controlled.
findings.expectedBehavior: referrerEmployeeId should default to the caller's own employee id (or be validated), and rewardAmount should be set by policy/HR, not the submitter.
findings.actualBehavior: Both fields are taken verbatim from the client at create.

evidence.snippet: create: protectedProcedure.input(createReferralSchema).mutation(async ({ ctx, input }) => { const [referral] = await ctx.db.insert(schema.tenant.referrals).values(input).returning(); return referral; })
referrerEmployeeId: z.string().uuid(),  ...  rewardAmount: z.number().min(0).optional(),  // both client-supplied

findings.reproduction: Call recruitment.referral.create with referrerEmployeeId = own id and rewardAmount = 10000. A referral crediting the caller is created (payout still requires HR).
findings.technicalRisk: Referrer attribution and proposed reward are not server-authoritative.
findings.businessRisk: Referral-bonus gaming if HR approves on trust; incorrect referrer-of-record.
findings.recommendation: Default referrerEmployeeId to the session employee (mirror the recognition.create SEC-010 fix) and derive rewardAmount from the reward policy rather than the client.
findings.workaround: HR verifies referrer and amount before marking reward_paid.

findings.title: Notifications: no unsubscribe / channel preferences; payslip email exposes net pay
findings.description: The notifications table supports email/sms/in_app channels but there is no user preference/unsubscribe surface — a data subject cannot opt out of a channel. The PayslipReady email template renders net pay ('{netPay} SAR') in the message body, so salary lands in the mail transport/inbox if/when dispatch is wired. Note: no email dispatch (resend.emails.send) is currently implemented anywhere, so templates are defined but unsent; this is both a functional gap and a reason to fix the payslip content before go-live.
findings.expectedBehavior: Per-user notification preferences/opt-out; sensitive emails avoid embedding salary figures (link to the app instead).
findings.actualBehavior: No preferences model; payslip email body includes net pay; no dispatch implemented.

evidence.snippet: <Text style={styles.netPayValue}>{netPay} SAR</Text>
export const notificationChannelEnum = ["email", "sms", "in_app"] as const;  // no user-preference table

findings.reproduction: Review notification router/schema for a preferences/unsubscribe surface (none); review payslip template for net pay.
findings.technicalRisk: Salary disclosure in email transport; no opt-out.
findings.businessRisk: PDPL data-minimisation and consent-management gaps once email is enabled.
findings.recommendation: Add a per-user notification-preferences table with opt-out and an unsubscribe path; remove the salary figure from the payslip email (link to the secured payslip instead) before wiring dispatch.
findings.workaround: Keep email dispatch disabled until content/preferences are addressed.

findings.title: Audit log retains salary old/new values as plaintext JSON
findings.description: For salary changes, writeAudit records old/new salary values in the audit_logs.newValue / oldValue jsonb columns in plaintext. This is inherent to an audit trail and access is restricted (HR Manager / super admin, tenant-scoped), but note the employee salary/PII fields are encrypted at rest in the employees table while their historical values sit unencrypted in audit_logs. Any DB-level exposure of audit_logs discloses salary history.
findings.expectedBehavior: Sensitive audit payloads are protected commensurately with the source columns (access control at minimum; consider encryption/tokenisation for salary snapshots).
findings.actualBehavior: Salary before/after values stored as plaintext jsonb in audit_logs.

evidence.snippet: oldValue: pickChanged(current as Record<string, unknown>, data), newValue: data,  // includes salary fields
oldValue: jsonb("old_value"), newValue: jsonb("new_value"),

findings.reproduction: Change a salary; inspect audit_logs row — salary values are plaintext.
findings.technicalRisk: DB compromise of audit_logs discloses salary history that is encrypted elsewhere.
findings.businessRisk: Residual PII exposure surface; inconsistent protection of the same data.
findings.recommendation: Restrict audit_logs at the DB layer; consider encrypting or masking salary snapshots in audit payloads, or storing field-change markers instead of raw values where full fidelity is not required.
findings.workaround: Ensure audit_logs access is tightly controlled and monitored.

findings.title: Only 3 Playwright e2e specs exist; no end-to-end coverage of payroll run, leave approval, or recruitment pipeline
findings.description: apps/web/e2e/ contains exactly 3 Playwright specs: auth.spec.ts, attendance-guide-map.spec.ts, attendance-reports.spec.ts (plus a non-test production-performance.mjs script). There is no e2e coverage for payroll run creation/approval, leave request/approval, employee onboarding, or the recruitment pipeline — all multi-step, high-value user flows.
findings.expectedBehavior: Critical multi-step flows (per CLAUDE.md's own testing rule requiring E2E for critical flows) should have at least a smoke-level Playwright spec.
findings.actualBehavior: E2E coverage is limited to auth and two attendance screens.

evidence.snippet: attendance-guide-map.spec.ts, attendance-reports.spec.ts, auth.spec.ts, production-performance.mjs — 3 spec files total

findings.reproduction: find apps/web/e2e -type f
findings.technicalRisk: UI regressions in payroll/leave/recruitment flows would not be caught by any automated browser test.
findings.businessRisk: Lower confidence that a UI refactor doesn't silently break the payroll run or leave approval flow before a release.
findings.recommendation: Add smoke-level Playwright specs for: leave request -> manager approval, payroll run creation -> approval -> payslip view, and recruitment candidate -> offer -> onboarding happy path.
findings.workaround: docs/e2e-status.md may already track this gap — cross-check before duplicating planning.

findings.title: Two router files exceed the project's own 800-line file-size guideline
findings.description: apps/web/trpc/routers/retention.ts is 1611 lines (2x the 800-line max) and recruitment.ts is 812 lines. Both are flat single-file routers covering many unrelated sub-domains (retention.ts alone spans goals, review cycles, reviews, skills, succession planning, engagement surveys, rewards, compensation, and talent reviews).
findings.expectedBehavior: Per the project's coding-style guideline (200-400 lines typical, 800 max), large routers should be split by sub-domain into separate files/modules.
findings.actualBehavior: retention.ts and recruitment.ts remain single monolithic files well past the stated maximum.

evidence.snippet: wc -l reports 1611 lines
wc -l reports 812 lines

findings.reproduction: find apps packages -name '*.ts' -o -name '*.tsx' | xargs wc -l | sort -rn | head
findings.technicalRisk: Large single files slow code review, increase merge-conflict likelihood, and make it harder to reason about which capability gate applies to which section.
findings.businessRisk: Slower feature velocity in the retention/recruitment modules as the team grows.
findings.recommendation: Split retention.ts into per-sub-domain router files (goal.ts, review.ts, skill.ts, succession.ts, engagement.ts, reward.ts, compensation.ts) merged via createTRPCRouter composition, mirroring the pattern already used for top-level routers in trpc/index.ts.
findings.workaround: None needed urgently; purely a maintainability concern.

findings.title: Leading-wildcard LIKE search on employees.fullName cannot use a standard index
findings.description: The employee search filter uses `like(schema.tenant.employees.fullName, \`%${input.search}%\`)` with a leading wildcard, which prevents a standard btree index from being used even if one existed (see QA-001) — Postgres would need a trigram (pg_trgm) or full-text index to serve this efficiently.
findings.expectedBehavior: Name search should use an index-friendly strategy (trigram index, or a prefix-only search) once tenant employee counts grow large.
findings.actualBehavior: Leading-wildcard LIKE forces a full scan of the filtered result set for the search term.

evidence.snippet: conditions.push(like(schema.tenant.employees.fullName, `%${input.search}%`));

findings.reproduction: Read apps/web/trpc/routers/employee.ts line 21.
findings.technicalRisk: Search-by-name degrades linearly with tenant employee count; combined with QA-001 (no index at all on employees), this compounds.
findings.businessRisk: Minor at current scale; would surface as a slow employee search for large enterprise tenants.
findings.recommendation: Add a pg_trgm GIN index on employees.full_name (CREATE EXTENSION pg_trgm; CREATE INDEX ... USING gin (full_name gin_trgm_ops)) if/when tenant sizes grow past a few thousand employees.
findings.workaround: Acceptable at current demo/pilot scale.

findings.title: recruitment.application.create and recruitment.referral.create trust full client payload on bare protectedProcedure (residual SEC-010)
findings.role: any staff role (payroll_admin, department_manager, hr_specialist included)
findings.description: SEC-010's create-spoofing sub-item was only partially remediated. offer.accept/decline, recognition.create/update and surveyResponse.create/update were fixed, but recruitment.application.create and recruitment.referral.create remain bare protectedProcedure that insert the raw client payload (including referrerEmployeeId) with no session binding and no recruitment:manage gate. Any staff role that passes canAccessProcedure (recruiter, but also payroll_admin, department_manager, hr_specialist) can create applications/referrals with a spoofed referrer to credit an arbitrary employee. It is not reachable by employee/candidate (not in their allowlists), so impact is staff-internal.
findings.expectedBehavior: Bind referrer/owner ids to the session where applicable and gate creation to requireRole(super_admin, hr_manager, recruiter) (recruitment:manage), consistent with the other recruitment mutations.
findings.actualBehavior: Staff roles without recruitment:manage can create applications/referrals with client-controlled owner fields.
findings.reproduction: Login as payroll_admin -> call recruitment.referral.create with an arbitrary referrerEmployeeId -> row created crediting that employee.
findings.technicalRisk: Data-integrity/spoofing on referral attribution and applications by roles lacking recruitment:manage.
findings.businessRisk: Referral-reward fraud potential; minor within-tenant integrity issue.
findings.recommendation: Change both to requireRole(super_admin, hr_manager, recruiter) and bind referrerEmployeeId to the session for self-referrals.
findings.workaround: None client-side.

findings.title: ai.assistant.list / getById remain companyProcedure (assistant config readable by all staff roles)
findings.description: Phase 2 moved ai.assistant.create/update/delete to requireCapability('settings:manage') but left the corresponding reads (ai.assistant.list, ai.assistant.getById) on companyProcedure, so any staff role can read the AI assistant configuration (prompts, model settings). Low sensitivity, but inconsistent with the settings:manage gating applied to the writes.
findings.expectedBehavior: Reads of settings-managed configuration should be requireCapability('settings:manage') (or at least align with who may manage them).
findings.actualBehavior: Any staff role reads AI assistant configuration.
findings.reproduction: Login as recruiter -> call ai.assistant.list -> returns assistant configs.
findings.technicalRisk: Low; configuration disclosure to over-broad staff set.
findings.businessRisk: Minor; prompt/config leakage within tenant.
findings.recommendation: Gate ai.assistant.list/getById on requireCapability('settings:manage') to match the write path.
findings.workaround: None client-side.

findings.title: Table rows use role="link" plus a nested real <button>, an unconventional interactive pattern
findings.wcagCriterion: 4.1.2 Name, Role, Value (A) — best-practice deviation, not a hard failure
findings.description: Each employee `<tr>` is given `role="link"`, `tabIndex={0}`, an `aria-label`, and both click and Enter/Space keydown handlers to navigate to the employee's profile — genuinely more accessible than a typical unlabeled div-onClick pattern, and this is the only such pattern found in a repo-wide scan for onClick divs (none found). However, `role="link"` on a `<tr>` that performs a client-side `router.push` (not a real href) is non-standard — links normally support right-click/open-in-new-tab and show a URL on hover/status bar, neither of which this provides. The row also contains a real nested `<Button>` ("View") that duplicates the row's own action, i.e. two overlapping interactive targets on one row.
findings.expectedBehavior: Either wrap the row's primary cell content in a real `<a href="/employees/{id}">` (progressively enhanced with client routing) so link semantics are accurate, or use `role="button"` if it must stay a synthetic control — and drop the redundant nested 'View' button once the row itself is fully operable.
findings.actualBehavior: Row exposes `role="link"` without real link affordances, plus a redundant nested button performing the same navigation.

evidence.snippet:
<TableRow
  key={employee.id}
  className="cursor-pointer focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-emerald-700/30"
  role="link"
  tabIndex={0}
  aria-label={`Open ${employee.fullName}`}
  onClick={() => router.push(`/employees/${employee.id}`)}

<Button variant="ghost" size="sm" onClick={(e) => { e.stopPropagation(); router.push(`/employees/${employee.id}`); }}>
  View
</Button>

findings.reproduction: Inspect any employee row's accessibility tree — role is 'link' but there is no href; a nested button with the same destination sits inside it.
findings.technicalRisk: Low; functionally keyboard-operable today (Enter/Space both handled), so this is a refinement rather than a blocker.
findings.businessRisk: Minor confusion for screen-reader users encountering a 'link' that behaves like a button, and slightly redundant navigation model.
findings.recommendation: Consider using `role="button"` (since it triggers a client-side action, not a navigable href) and drop the duplicate 'View' button, or convert the whole row to a real anchor for proper link semantics.
findings.workaround: Row is already keyboard operable via Enter/Space; not blocking.

findings.title: Raw SQL string interpolation in demo seed endpoint
findings.description: apps/web/app/api/seed/demo-data/route.ts builds INSERT/UPDATE statements by string-interpolating values (emp.id, t.id, balance, year, department ids) directly into SQL passed to adminDb.execute(). The interpolated values are trusted (UUIDs/numbers read from the DB, not user input) and the endpoint is guarded by a NODE_ENV!=production check plus MIGRATION_TOKEN, so exploitability is low - but the pattern is an injection-prone anti-pattern and the only string-concatenated SQL in the codebase alongside the migrate routes.
findings.expectedBehavior: All queries should use Drizzle's parameterized builder or sql`` with bound parameters, never raw string interpolation - per the project's 'never raw SQL' convention (CLAUDE.md).
findings.actualBehavior: Values are concatenated into raw SQL strings via .replace() and template literals.

evidence.snippet: await adminDb.execute(`INSERT INTO ${schema}.leave_balances (id, employee_id, leave_type_id, balance, year) VALUES (gen_random_uuid(), '${emp.id}', '${t.id}', ${balance}, ${year})`);

findings.reproduction: Not remotely exploitable in production (blocked by NODE_ENV guard + token); flagged as an insecure pattern / defense-in-depth gap.
findings.technicalRisk: If any interpolated source ever becomes attacker-influenced, this becomes SQL injection.
findings.businessRisk: Low while values are DB-derived and endpoint is demo-only.
findings.recommendation: Delete the demo seed/migrate endpoints after the demo (as their own comments state), or rewrite using parameterized Drizzle inserts.
findings.workaround: Ensure NODE_ENV=production in all live environments (currently the case) and remove the endpoints post-demo.

findings.title: Unauthenticated /api/vitals accepts partially-validated payload (crash + log injection)
findings.description: /api/vitals is unauthenticated (acceptable for a beacon) and validates payload.name against an allowlist, but does not validate that payload.value is a number before calling payload.value.toFixed(2) - a non-numeric value throws outside the try/catch, yielding an unhandled 500. payload.path is logged unsanitized (log-injection vector). Low sensitivity, but input is not fully validated (no Zod).
findings.expectedBehavior: Validate the whole payload with Zod (name enum, value number, bounded path) before use.
findings.actualBehavior: Only name is checked; value type and path content are trusted.

evidence.snippet: if (payload.rating === "poor") { console.warn("[vitals][poor]", payload.name, payload.value.toFixed(2), payload.path ?? ""); }  // value assumed numeric; path unsanitized

findings.reproduction: POST /api/vitals {"name":"LCP","value":"x","rating":"poor"} -> value.toFixed throws -> 500; or set path to a CRLF string to inject log lines.
findings.technicalRisk: Unhandled exception; log injection.
findings.businessRisk: Negligible; log integrity only.
findings.recommendation: Validate the payload with a Zod schema (value: z.number(), path bounded/stripped of control chars) and wrap logging in the try/catch.
findings.workaround: Coerce/guard value and strip control characters from path.

findings.title: Vercel OIDC JWT tokens stored in local .env.local / .env.production.local
findings.description: VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN (a signed JWT) is present in both .env.local and .env.production.local. These files are gitignored and the tokens are short-lived (exp claim ~12h from iat), so residual risk is low, but they are auto-generated bearer tokens sitting on disk.
findings.expectedBehavior: Ephemeral OIDC tokens are fine when gitignored and short-lived; ensure they are never committed or shared.
findings.actualBehavior: Tokens present on disk (gitignored, not committed).

evidence.snippet: VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN="eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVC....(short-lived JWT)"

findings.reproduction: Open .env.local / .env.production.local.
findings.technicalRisk: If a token is exfiltrated before expiry it could be replayed against Vercel OIDC-scoped resources.
findings.businessRisk: Low; time-boxed.
findings.recommendation: No action beyond confirming .gitignore coverage (already present). Do not paste these files into shared channels.
findings.workaround: N/A

findings.title: Password minimum length of 8 and no breached-password / max-length-independent checks
findings.description: The shared password policy requires 8+ chars with upper/lower/digit/special (good composition, and a 128 max that safely bounds bcrypt input). For a product holding payroll/PII, an 8-char minimum is on the low side; there is no check against known-breached passwords (e.g. HaveIBeenPwned k-anonymity) and no MFA-required compensating control for admins (see AUTH-006). Note bcrypt truncates input at 72 bytes - acceptable here given the 128-char cap and complexity, but worth documenting.
findings.expectedBehavior: Raise the minimum to 12 for privileged roles and/or add a breached-password screen; document the bcrypt 72-byte behavior.
findings.actualBehavior: 8-char minimum, no breach check.

evidence.snippet:
export const passwordSchema = z
  .string()
  .min(8, "Password must be at least 8 characters")
  .max(128, "Password is too long")
  .regex(/[A-Z]/, ...)

findings.reproduction: Register with an 8-char password like 'Passw0r!' - accepted.
findings.technicalRisk: Slightly weaker credentials for admin accounts.
findings.businessRisk: Marginal.
findings.recommendation: Increase minimum length (12 for privileged roles), add a breached-password check, and pair with enforced MFA for admins.
findings.workaround: Enforce MFA for privileged roles (AUTH-006).

findings.title: Deterministic (SIV) field encryption leaks plaintext equality — deliberate, documented tradeoff for the iqama unique index
findings.exploitability: An observer of ciphertext can tell which rows share the same plaintext (e.g., two employees with the same IBAN) but cannot recover the plaintext.
findings.description: encryptField derives the AES-GCM IV as HMAC(ivKey, plaintext) truncated to 12 bytes (a synthetic-IV/SIV construction), making ciphertext deterministic. This is required so the iqama partial unique index and equality lookups work on the encrypted column. The known cost is that equal plaintexts produce equal ciphertexts (equality is observable). The code is a correct authenticated-encryption construction: AES-256-GCM, 16-byte tag, domain-separated enc/iv subkeys via HMAC labels, tamper rejection on read (verified by crypto.test.ts). Recording as a strength with a documented caveat, not a defect.
findings.expectedBehavior: For a column needing a unique index, deterministic encryption is the accepted design; equality leakage is inherent and acknowledged in code comments.
findings.actualBehavior: As designed. Note: fields that do NOT need equality/uniqueness (e.g., bank_iban_enc, and mfa_secret per DB-011) would be better served by randomized IVs to avoid unnecessary equality leakage.

evidence.snippet: // The IV is derived from the plaintext ... making the ciphertext DETERMINISTIC ... The trade-off is that equality of plaintexts is observable ... acceptable and necessary here.

findings.reproduction: encryptField('X') === encryptField('X') (true, by design); different plaintexts differ.
findings.technicalRisk: Equality-pattern leakage on encrypted columns; low sensitivity for identifiers already unique.
findings.businessRisk: Minimal; frequency analysis only reveals duplicate values.
findings.recommendation: Keep deterministic encryption for iqama (unique-index requirement). For fields not needing equality (IBAN, MFA secret), consider a randomized-IV variant to remove equality leakage. Document the field-by-field choice.
findings.workaround: None required.

findings.title: Consent capture labelled 'GDPR' in a Saudi PDPL product
findings.description: Candidate creation captures explicit consent (a genuine strength), but the field and validation message are named for GDPR while the product's governing regime is Saudi PDPL. The consent language/basis should reference PDPL to be accurate for the jurisdiction.
findings.expectedBehavior: Consent wording and field naming reference the applicable PDPL basis.
findings.actualBehavior: Field named gdprConsent with a GDPR-worded validation message.

evidence.snippet: e.gdprConsent = "GDPR consent is required to store candidate data";
I confirm that this candidate has provided explicit consent to have their

findings.reproduction: Open candidate create form; consent label references GDPR.
findings.technicalRisk: None.
findings.businessRisk: Consent record may not map to PDPL lawful-basis requirements; audit optics.
findings.recommendation: Rename to reflect PDPL and have legal confirm the consent wording/basis for KSA.
findings.workaround: N/A

findings.title: Debug endpoint /api/auth/debug leaks env/config metadata (production-gated)
findings.description: GET /api/auth/debug returns AUTH_SECRET length, AUTH_URL/NEXTAUTH_URL values, DB host/port, NODE_ENV and DEMO_MODE. It is correctly disabled in production (returns 404 unless ENABLE_DEBUG_ENDPOINT === 'true'), so risk is limited to non-production environments. Recorded for completeness; if a non-prod environment is internet-reachable this leaks the auth-secret length and DB host.
findings.expectedBehavior: Diagnostic endpoints should not be shipped as public HTTP routes; if kept, restrict by network/auth even outside production.
findings.actualBehavior: In non-production, anonymous callers receive env/config metadata.
findings.reproduction: In a non-prod deployment: curl /api/auth/debug -> 200 JSON with authSecretLength, dbHost, etc.
findings.technicalRisk: Minor metadata disclosure feeding secret/side-channel guessing in non-prod.
findings.businessRisk: Low.
findings.recommendation: Delete the route or require an operator token even in non-production; never expose AUTH_SECRET length or DB host.
findings.workaround: Ensure NODE_ENV=production and ENABLE_DEBUG_ENDPOINT unset in all internet-reachable deployments.
